topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT632 - MICRO-ELECTRO-MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Microsensors: Acoustic Wave Sensors, Biomedical Sensors and Biosensors, Chemical Sensors, Pressure Sensors, Optical Sensors, Microfluidics ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What are MEMS Microsensors? A **MEMS Microsensor** is a microfabricated device that converts a physical, chemical, biological, or optical stimulus into a measurable electrical signal. In the KTU 2024 scheme, microsensors are classified based on the **energy domain** they transduce from (mechanical, chemical, biological, optical) to the electrical domain. They are the *sensing counterpart* of MEMS actuators. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A microsensor is a device that detects a change in a physical quantity (pressure, mass, temperature, chemical concentration) and converts it into a processable signal, typically electrical, using microfabrication techniques such as bulk or surface micromachining. ### 1.2 Classification of Microsensors (Module 2 Scope) The KTU 2024 syllabus (PEERT632, Module 2) requires mastery of the following six sub-classes: | # | Sensor Type | Input Stimulus | Output Domain | Typical Applications | |---|-------------|----------------|---------------|----------------------| | 1 | Acoustic Wave Sensors | Mass, viscosity, humidity | Frequency shift | Gas detection, biosensing | | 2 | Biomedical Sensors | Pressure, flow, bioelectric | Voltage/Resistance | Pacemakers, blood pressure | | 3 | Biosensors | Biochemical interaction | Current/Voltage | Glucose monitoring, DNA detection | | 4 | Chemical Sensors | Gas/liquid concentration | Resistance/Potential | pH meters, toxic gas detection | | 5 | Pressure Sensors | Mechanical force | Capacitance/Piezo-voltage | Altimeters, tire pressure | | 6 | Optical Sensors | Light intensity/wavelength | Photocurrent/Voltage | Displays, spectrometers | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator at the Border" Imagine each sensor as a **specialized translator** standing at a border between two countries (the physical world and the electronics world). A pressure sensor translates "pushes" into "voltages," an acoustic sensor translates "tiny vibrations" into "frequency changes," and a biosensor translates "sugar molecules landing on a surface" into "tiny currents." MEMS technology simply makes these translators microscopic, mass-producible like chips, and ultra-low-power. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Always identify the **transduction principle** first. Examiners award marks for explicitly stating *what physical effect converts input to output* (e.g., piezoelectric effect, piezoresistive effect, fluorescence). ### 1.4 Generalized Sensor Performance Metrics - **Sensitivity (S):** Ratio of output change to input change. Units depend on sensor type (Hz/μg, V/kPa, A/W). - **Selectivity:** Ability to respond only to the target stimulus in the presence of interferents. - **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change in input. - **Dynamic Range:** Ratio of maximum to minimum measurable input, often expressed in **dB**. - **Limit of Detection (LOD):** The lowest concentration/quantity that produces a signal-to-noise ratio ≥ 3. - **Response Time (τ):** Time required to reach 63.2% (i.e., $1 - e^{-1}$) of the steady-state output. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** For biosensors and chemical sensors, *selectivity* is the most heavily weighted grading parameter in KTU valuation, often worth 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Acoustic Wave Sensors (AWS) #### 2.1.1 Operating Principle Acoustic wave sensors exploit the propagation of mechanical waves (typically **Rayleigh waves**, **Love waves**, or **bulk acoustic waves**) through a piezoelectric substrate. A mass or viscosity change on the wave path alters the wave's velocity, which is measured as a **frequency shift** ($\Delta f$). #### 2.1.2 Types of Acoustic Wave Sensors 1. **BAW (Bulk Acoustic Wave) / QCM (Quartz Crystal Microbalance):** Thickness-shear mode; frequency range **5–30 MHz**. 2. **SAW (Surface Acoustic Wave):** Rayleigh waves on a piezoelectric surface; frequency range **50 MHz – 2.5 GHz**. 3. **FBAR (Film Bulk Acoustic Resonator):** Thin-film piezoelectric resonators; used in mobile RF filters. 4. **Love Wave Sensors:** Shear-horizontal wave guided by a guiding layer; ideal for liquid sensing (avoids acoustic radiation into liquid). #### 2.1.3 The Sauerbrey Equation (High-Yield Formula) For a QCM operating in gas phase, the mass added per unit area is directly proportional to the frequency shift: $$\Delta f = -\frac{2 f_0^2}{A \sqrt{\mu_q \rho_q}} \cdot \Delta m$$ Where: - $\Delta f$ = frequency shift in **Hz** - $f_0$ = fundamental resonant frequency of the crystal - $A$ = active electrode area (m²) - $\mu_q$ = shear modulus of quartz (= $2.947 \times 10^{10}$ N/m²) - $\rho_q$ = density of quartz (= **2648 kg/m³**) - $\Delta m$ = mass change per unit area (μg/cm²) > [!NOTE] > The negative sign indicates that added mass *decreases* the resonant frequency. ### 2.2 Biomedical Sensors & Biosensors #### 2.2.1 Biomedical Microsensors These sense physiological parameters *in vivo* or *in vitro*. Key examples: - **Intravenous pressure sensors:** Piezoresistive diaphragms; range **0–300 mmHg**. - **Catheter-tip flow sensors:** Hot-wire anemometry at the μm scale. - **Neural probes:** Microelectrode arrays detecting action potentials ($\sim 100 \mu V$, $1$ kHz). #### 2.2.2 Biosensors — The "Lock and Key" Concept A biosensor has three components: 1. **Bio-receptor** (enzyme, antibody, DNA strand, whole cell) — provides *selectivity*. 2. **Transducer** (electrochemical, optical, piezoelectric) — provides *sensitivity*. 3. **Electronics** (amplifier, ADC) — provides *readability*. **The Classic Glucose Biosensor (Clark & Updike, 1967):** Uses the enzyme **glucose oxidase (GOx)** immobilized on a platinum electrode. Reaction: $$ \text{Glucose} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GOx}} \text{Gluconic Acid} + H_2O_2 $$ The electrode measures either the $O_2$ consumed or the $H_2O_2$ produced, both proportional to glucose concentration. ### 2.3 Chemical Sensors Chemical sensors convert the concentration of a chemical species into an electrical signal. Major families: | Type | Transduction | Selectivity Mechanism | |------|--------------|----------------------| | Metal-Oxide (MOS) | Resistance change | Surface chemisorption | | Electrochemical | Current at electrode | Redox potential | | Amperometric | Current at fixed V | Faraday's law | | Potentiometric | Voltage at zero current | Nernst equation | | ChemFET / ISFET | Threshold voltage shift | Ion-sensitive gate | **The Nernst Equation** (for potentiometric sensors): $$ E = E_0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(a_{\text{ion}}) $$ At standard temperature ($T = 298$ K), this simplifies to: $$ E = E_0 + \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10}(a_{\text{ion}}) \quad \text{[V]} $$ Where $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**, $F$ = **96,485 C/mol**, $n$ = electron count. ### 2.4 Pressure Sensors #### 2.4.1 Operating Principle A thin micromachined diaphragm deflects under differential pressure. The deflection is converted to an electrical signal using one of three methods: 1. **Piezoresistive:** Strain gauges whose resistance changes with stress. 2. **Capacitive:** Plate separation changes capacitance. 3. **Piezoelectric:** Stress generates a charge. #### 2.4.2 Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor Equations The fractional resistance change of a diffused gauge is: $$ \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t $$ For a square diaphragm of side $a$ and thickness $h$ under pressure $P$, the maximum stress at the edge is: $$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{0.308 \, P \, a^2}{h^2} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **highest-yield formula** for KTU pressure sensor problems. Examiners love to ask students to plot $\sigma$ vs. $P$ for given dimensions. #### 2.4.3 Capacitive Pressure Sensor For a parallel-plate capacitor with diaphragm as the moving plate: $$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - w(P)} $$ Where $w(P)$ is the pressure-dependent center deflection of the diaphragm. Sensitivity: $$ S_C = \frac{\partial C}{\partial P} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A \, \partial w/\partial P}{(d - w)^2} $$ ### 2.5 Optical Sensors (MEMS) MEMS optical sensors include: - **Photodiodes / Phototransistors** — convert photons to current. - **DMD (Digital Micromirror Device)** — Texas Instruments' millions of tilting mirrors. - **MOEMS spectrometers** — micro-gratings + micro-mirrors. - **Optical pressure sensors** — Fabry-Pérot interferometers. **Responsivity (R) of a photodiode:** $$ R = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} \quad [\text{A/W}] $$ Where $\eta$ = quantum efficiency, $q$ = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $h$ = Planck's constant, $c$ = speed of light. ### 2.6 Microfluidics #### 2.6.1 Definition Microfluidics is the science of manipulating fluids (typically $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-18}$ L) in channels with dimensions of **1–1000 μm**. #### 2.6.2 Key Dimensionless Numbers | Number | Formula | Physical Meaning | |--------|---------|------------------| | Reynolds | $Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Inertial vs. viscous forces | | Capillary | $Ca = \frac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ | Viscous vs. surface tension | | Bond | $Bo = \frac{\rho g L^2}{\gamma}$ | Gravity vs. surface tension | | Péclet | $Pe = \frac{v L}{D}$ | Convection vs. diffusion | > [!IMPORTANT] > In most microfluidic systems, $Re < 100$ (typically $Re \sim 1$). Flow is **laminar** and dominated by viscosity, not inertia. This is the *single most important fact* in microfluidics for KTU exams. #### 2.6.3 Hagen-Poiseuille Flow in a Rectangular Microchannel For pressure-driven flow in a rectangular channel of width $w$, height $h$, and length $L$: $$ Q = \frac{h^3 w}{12 \mu L} \left( 1 - 0.63 \frac{h}{w} \right) \Delta P \quad \text{for } h \leq w $$ The hydraulic diameter for a rectangular channel: $$ D_h = \frac{2wh}{w + h} $$ ### 2.7 Consolidated KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Domain | Formula | Key Variables | Units | |--------|---------|---------------|-------| | Acoustic (Sauerbrey) | $\Delta f = -\dfrac{2 f_0^2}{A \sqrt{\mu_q \rho_q}} \Delta m$ | $f_0, A, \Delta m$ | Hz, m², μg/cm² | | Chemical (Nernst) | $E = E_0 + \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log(a)$ | $E_0, n, a$ | V, dimensionless | | Pressure (Diaphragm) | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{0.308 P a^2}{h^2}$ | $P, a, h$ | Pa, m, m | | Capacitive | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - w}$ | $\varepsilon_0, A, d, w$ | F, m², m, m | | Optical | $R = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | $\eta, \lambda$ | A/W, nm | | Microfluidic | $Q = \dfrac{h^3 w \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ | $h, w, \mu, L$ | m³/s, m, Pa·s, m | | Reynolds | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | $\rho, v, L, \mu$ | kg/m³, m/s, m, Pa·s | ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Acoustic Wave Sensors:** Used as the chemical "nose" in airport explosives detectors. - **Biosensors:** Continuous glucose monitors (CGM) for diabetes patients (~$5B global market). - **Chemical Sensors:** Carbon monoxide detectors in homes, alcohol breathalyzers. - **Pressure Sensors:** Barometric altimeters in smartphones, MEMS microphones. - **Optical Sensors:** LiDAR in autonomous vehicles, fiber-optic gyroscopes. - **Microfluidics:** Lab-on-chip for PCR testing (critical during pandemics), inkjet printheads. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Deflection of a Square Pressure-Sensor Diaphragm Consider a square diaphragm of side $a$ and thickness $h$, clamped on all four edges, subjected to a uniform pressure $P$ on its top surface. **Step 1: Governing differential equation (Plate Theory)** For small deflections, the plate equation is: $$ D \nabla^4 w = P $$ Where $D$ is the flexural rigidity: $$ D = \frac{E h^3}{12 (1 - \nu^2)} $$ **Step 2: Boundary conditions (fully clamped)** At the edges ($x = 0, a$ and $y = 0, a$): $$ w = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial n} = 0 $$ **Step 3: Series solution (Galerkin approximation)** Assume a trial function satisfying the boundary conditions: $$ w(x,y) = w_0 \cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\pi y}{a}\right) $$ **Step 4: Apply Galerkin method** $$ \iint D \nabla^4 w \cdot \cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\pi y}{a}\right) \, dx \, dy = P \iint \cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\pi y}{a}\right) \, dx \, dy $$ **Step 5: Compute the operator** $$ \nabla^4 \cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\pi y}{a}\right) = \left(\frac{\pi}{a}\right)^4 \cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\pi y}{a}\right) $$ **Step 6: Integrate** Both integrals yield the factor $\frac{a^2}{4}$ which cancels, leaving: $$ D \left(\frac{\pi}{a}\right)^4 w_0 = P \implies w_0 = \frac{P a^4}{D \pi^4} $$ **Step 7: Substitute $D$** $$ w_0 = \frac{12 (1 - \nu^2) P a^4}{E h^3 \pi^4} $$ **Step 8: Maximum stress derivation** The bending moments are: $$ M_x = -D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right) $$ The maximum bending stress at the clamped edge midspan (where $y = 0, x = a/2$): $$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{6 M_x}{h^2} = \frac{0.308 \, P a^2}{h^2} $$ The numerical coefficient **0.308** arises from $\dfrac{6 \pi^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{...}$ integrated over the clamped edge with $\nu = 0.3$ (silicon Poisson ratio). ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensitivity of a Capacitive Pressure Sensor **Step 1: Capacitance at zero pressure** With a fixed gap $d_0$ and area $A$: $$ C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0} $$ **Step 2: Apply pressure $P$ → center deflection $w_0$** Using the result of Derivation 3.1: $$ w_0 = \frac{0.00126 \, P a^4}{E h^3} \quad (\text{for } \nu = 0.3) $$ Where the coefficient is $\dfrac{12(1-0.09)}{E \pi^4} \approx 0.00126 / E$. **Step 3: New capacitance (small-deflection approximation)** For $w_0 \ll d_0$, the gap becomes $d(x,y) = d_0 - w(x,y)$: $$ C(P) = \iint \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - w(x,y)} \, dx \, dy $$ **Step 4: First-order Taylor expansion** $$ C(P) \approx C_0 \left( 1 + \frac{\bar{w}}{d_0} \right) $$ Where $\bar{w}$ is the average deflection across the diaphragm. Using the parabolic shape of a uniformly loaded clamped plate, $\bar{w} = w_0 / 3.4$ approximately. **Step 5: Sensitivity** $$ S = \frac{\Delta C / C_0}{P} = \frac{w_0 / (3.4 \, d_0)}{P} = \frac{0.00126 \, a^4}{3.4 \, E h^3 d_0} $$ Substituting $E_{\text{Si}} = 170$ GPa: $$ S = \frac{2.18 \times 10^{-15} \, a^4}{h^3 d_0} \quad \text{[per Pa]} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Resonance Frequency Shift of a Cantilever-Based Mass Sensor A cantilever of length $L$, width $b$, thickness $h$, density $\rho$, with a point mass $M$ at its tip. **Step 1: Euler-Bernoulli beam equation** $$ EI \frac{\partial^4 y}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = 0 $$ **Step 2: Free-clamped boundary conditions** At $x = 0$ (clamped): $y(0) = 0$ and $y'(0) = 0$. At $x = L$ (free): $y''(L) = 0$ and $y'''(L) = 0$. **Step 3: Mode shape solution** The eigenvalues $\lambda_n$ satisfy $\cos(\lambda_n L) \cosh(\lambda_n L) = -1$, giving the first mode $\lambda_1 L \approx 1.875$. **Step 4: Natural frequency of unloaded cantilever** $$ f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}} = 0.162 \frac{h}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} $$ **Step 5: Add tip mass and apply Rayleigh's method** The effective mass of the cantilever is $m_{\text{eff}} = 0.24 \, \rho A L$. The new frequency is: $$ f_1' = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{(\rho A L + M) L^3}} $$ **Step 6: Frequency shift for small added mass ($M \ll \rho A L$)** $$ \frac{\Delta f}{f_1} = -\frac{M}{2(\rho A L + M)} \approx -\frac{M}{2 m_{\text{eff}}} = -\frac{M}{0.48 \, \rho A L} $$ This negative shift is the basis of **mass-sensitive acoustic wave sensors**. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection Decision Tree | Application | Recommended Sensor | Justification | |-------------|-------------------|---------------| | Detect 1 ppb of $NO_2$ in air | Metal-oxide chemical sensor | Resistance change upon chemisorption | | Monitor blood glucose continuously | Amperometric biosensor (GOx) | Enzymatic specificity | | Tire pressure monitoring (TPMS) | Piezoresistive pressure sensor | Low cost, low power | | Detect 1 ng of bacteria on a chip | Love-wave acoustic sensor | Operates in liquid; high $Q$ | | Spectrometer on a smartphone | MOEMS grating + photodiode | Compact, low-power | | Single-cell sorting | Dielectrophoresis in microfluidics | Field gradient at μm scale | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic MEMS Sensor Architecture ```mermaid graph TD node1[Physical Stimulus
Pressure Mass Light Chemical] node2[Microstructure
Diaphragm Cantilever Membrane] node3[Transduction Layer
Piezoresistive Piezoelectric Capacitive Optical] node4[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier Filter ADC] node5[Digital Output
Voltage Current Frequency] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node2 -.Feedback.-> node1 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Biosensor Functional Flow ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Analyte in Sample
Blood Saliva Air] nodeB[Bio Recognition Element
Enzyme Antibody DNA] nodeC[Transducer
Electrochemical Optical Piezoelectric] nodeD[Electronics
Op-amp ADC] nodeE[Display / Data Logger] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Microfluidic Lab-on-Chip System ```mermaid graph TD nodeIn[Sample Inlet
Microchannel 100um wide] nodePump[Micropump
Peristaltic or Electroosmotic] nodeMix[Passive Micromixer
Serpentine Channel] nodeReact[Micro Reaction Chamber
Functionalized Surface] nodeDetect[On chip Detector
Optical or Electrochemical] nodeWaste[Waste Outlet] nodeIn --> nodePump nodePump --> nodeMix nodeMix --> nodeReact nodeReact --> nodeDetect nodeDetect --> nodeWaste ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor Cross-Section ```mermaid graph TD nodeTop[Top Surface
Reference Pressure] nodeDiaph[Silicon Diaphragm
Thickness 5 to 50 um] nodeGauges[4 Piezoresistors
Wheatstone Bridge] nodeCavity[Sealed Vacuum Cavity
Absolute Reference] nodeSub[Silicon Substrate] nodeTop --> nodeDiaph nodeDiaph --> nodeGauges nodeGauges --> nodeCavity nodeCavity --> nodeSub ``` ### 4.5 Block Diagram — Acoustic Wave Sensor Operating Principle ```mermaid graph LR nodeIDT1[Input IDT
Interdigitated Electrode] nodePiezo[piezoelectric Substrate
Quartz or LiNbO3] nodeWave[Acoustic Wave
Rayleigh or Love] nodeMass[Mass Loading on Surface
Analyte Adsorption] nodeIDT2[Output IDT] nodeOsc[Oscillator Circuit] nodeOsc --> nodeIDT1 nodeIDT1 --> nodePiezo nodePiezo --> nodeWave nodeWave --> nodeMass nodeMass --> nodeWave nodeWave --> nodeIDT2 nodeIDT2 --> nodeOsc ``` ### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Multi-Sensor MEMS Platform ```mermaid graph TD nodeCore[MEMS Multi Modal Sensor Platform] nodeA[Acoustic Wave Subsystem] nodeB[Biosensor Subsystem] nodeC[Chemical Sensor Subsystem] nodeD[Pressure Sensor Subsystem] nodeE[Optical Sensor Subsystem] nodeF[Microfluidic Delivery Network] nodeMCU[Microcontroller and ADC] nodeBLE[Bluetooth or Wireless TX] nodeA --> nodeMCU nodeB --> nodeMCU nodeC --> nodeMCU nodeD --> nodeMCU nodeE --> nodeMCU nodeF --> nodeA nodeF --> nodeB nodeF --> nodeC nodeMCU --> nodeBLE ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define a biosensor. List its three essential components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **biosensor** is an analytical device that integrates a biological recognition element with a physicochemical transducer to detect a target analyte. The three essential components are: 1. **Bio-receptor** (e.g., enzyme, antibody, nucleic acid) — provides *selectivity*. 2. **Transducer** (electrochemical, optical, piezoelectric) — converts the biological event into a measurable signal. 3. **Electronic reader** (amplifier, ADC, display) — processes and displays the output. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for naming the three components with one-line roles.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **State the Sauerbrey equation and explain the physical significance of the negative sign.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$ \Delta f = -\frac{2 f_0^2}{A \sqrt{\mu_q \rho_q}} \cdot \Delta m $$ - $f_0$ = fundamental frequency of the quartz crystal. - $A$ = area of the electrode. - $\Delta m$ = mass deposited per unit area. - The **negative sign** indicates that the resonant frequency *decreases* as mass is added to the sensor surface (loading effect), i.e., the output signal shifts downward in frequency. --- ### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **piezoresistive pressure sensor**. Derive the expression for the maximum bending stress in a square diaphragm. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) sensor** for chemical gas detection. State and explain the Sauerbrey equation. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 marks **Block Diagram & Construction (3 marks):** A piezoresistive pressure sensor consists of: - A **silicon diaphragm** micromachined by anisotropic etching (e.g., KOH or DRIE). - **Four piezoresistors** diffused near the edges of the diaphragm (where stress is maximum). - These four resistors are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. - A sealed reference cavity below the diaphragm (vacuum for absolute pressure sensors). - Bond pads for electrical connection. **Working Principle (2 marks):** Applied pressure causes the diaphragm to deflect, generating tensile and compressive stresses at its edges. Piezoresistors in tensile regions increase in resistance; those in compressive regions decrease. The Wheatstone bridge converts this differential resistance change into a voltage output: $$ V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \Delta R / R} \approx V_{\text{ex}} \frac{\Delta R}{R} $$ **Derivation of Maximum Stress (2 marks):** For a square diaphragm of side $a$ and thickness $h$ under uniform pressure $P$ with Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$: $$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{0.308 \, P \, a^2}{h^2} \quad [\text{Pa}] $$ The derivation uses plate theory with clamped boundary conditions and the Galerkin method (as detailed in Section 3.1). The maximum stress occurs at the midpoints of the clamped edges. --- ### Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 marks **SAW Sensor Construction (3 marks):** A SAW sensor consists of: - A **piezoelectric substrate** (quartz, LiNbO₃, or GaAs). - **Two Inter-Digitated Transducers (IDTs)** — input and output — photolithographically patterned metal combs. - A **sensitive coating** (e.g., polymer, functionalized layer) between the IDTs that selectively absorbs the target gas. **Working Principle (2 marks):** 1. An AC signal applied to the input IDT generates a **Rayleigh surface acoustic wave** via the converse piezoelectric effect. 2. The wave propagates along the substrate, accumulates a phase shift and amplitude change due to mass loading / viscosity change from adsorbed analyte. 3. The output IDT reconverts this mechanical wave back to an electrical signal. 4. The oscillator circuit's frequency shifts in proportion to the adsorbed mass. **Sauerbrey Equation (2 marks):** $$ \Delta f = -\frac{2 f_0^2}{A \sqrt{\mu_q \rho_q}} \cdot \Delta m $$ This equation shows that the **frequency shift is proportional to the mass loading per unit area**, making SAW sensors highly sensitive mass detectors (ng-level LOD achievable). --- ### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 **Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** Describe the working of a **glucose biosensor** with a neat diagram. Write the biochemical reaction involved. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **principle of microfluidics** with reference to Reynolds and Capillary numbers. Why is laminar flow dominant in microchannels? **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Q4(a) — 7 marks **Introduction (1 mark):** The glucose biosensor, also called the **Clark glucose electrode**, uses the enzyme **glucose oxidase (GOx)** as the bio-recognition element. **Construction (2 marks):** - A **platinum working electrode** with immobilized GOx. - An **Ag/AgCl reference electrode**. - An electrochemical cell (three-electrode configuration). - A potentiostat circuit applies 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl. **Working & Reaction (3 marks):** When blood/sample is applied, the following enzymatic reaction occurs: $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GOx}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_6 \text{ (Gluconic Acid)} + H_2O_2 $$ The $H_2O_2$ produced is oxidized at the platinum electrode: $$ H_2O_2 \xrightarrow{+0.6\,V} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- $$ The resulting current is proportional to the glucose concentration (typically $0.5–10$ nA per mg/dL). **Advantages (1 mark):** High selectivity, fast response ($\sim 30$ s), low sample volume (μL). --- ### Model Solution for Q4(b) — 7 marks **Microfluidics Definition (1 mark):** Microfluidics is the science of manipulating nanoliter-to-picoliter volumes of fluids in channels with characteristic dimensions from **1 to 1000 μm**. **Reynolds Number (2 marks):** $$ Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} $$ - $\rho$ = fluid density, $v$ = velocity, $L$ = characteristic length, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity. - In microchannels, $L \sim 100 \,\mu m$ and $v \sim 1$ mm/s, giving $Re \sim 10^{-2}$ to $10^{0}$. - **$Re \ll 2000$** ⟹ flow is **laminar**, viscous effects dominate. **Capillary Number (2 marks):** $$ Ca = \frac{\mu v}{\gamma} $$ - $\gamma$ = surface tension (≈ $0.072$ N/m for water). - At the microscale, $Ca$ is small ⟹ **surface tension dominates over viscous forces**, enabling capillary-driven flow (no pump needed). **Why Laminar Flow Dominates (2 marks):** 1. The characteristic length $L$ is small, so the inertial term $\rho v^2$ scales as $L^2$ while viscous term $\mu v / L$ scales as $L$. Viscous forces dominate as $L \to 0$. 2. Mixing in laminar flow occurs only by **diffusion**, which is slow but predictable — exploited in laminar diffusion-based separation and gradient generation. 3. This predictability enables **precise fluid handling** for lab-on-chip diagnostics. --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting units** in the Sauerbrey equation — $A$ must be in m², $\Delta m$ in kg/m², frequency in Hz. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units. > 2. **Mixing up Nernst vs. Butler-Volmer equations** — Nernst is for *equilibrium* (potentiometric); Butler-Volmer is for *kinetics* (amperometric). Wrong application = –1 mark. > 3. **Stating "pressure sensor uses piezoelectric effect"** without specifying whether it is *piezoelectric* (charge) or *piezoresistive* (resistance change). Examiners mark this strictly. Be precise. > 4. **Ignoring the "negative sign"** in the Sauerbrey equation. Mentioning the physical meaning of the sign (frequency decreases with added mass) often secures the full mark. > 5. **In pressure-sensor stress derivation**, do not omit the numerical coefficient (0.308). Showing the clamped-edge boundary condition explicitly is required for full marks. > 6. **Microfluidics question trap:** Many students confuse Reynolds number definition. State the formula *and* substitute realistic microfluidic values to demonstrate $Re \ll 1$. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **MEMS microsensor** converts a physical/chemical/biological stimulus into an electrical signal. - ✅ The **Sauerbrey equation** relates frequency shift to mass loading on a QCM/SAW sensor; the negative sign indicates a downward frequency shift. - ✅ **Acoustic wave sensors** (BAW, SAW, FBAR, Love-wave) are mass-sensitive devices with LOD down to pg/cm². - ✅ **Biosensors** have three components: bio-receptor, transducer, and electronics. The glucose biosensor uses GOx and measures $H_2O_2$ oxidation current. - ✅ **Chemical sensors** are governed by the **Nernst equation** (potentiometric) with a 59.2 mV/decade slope at 298 K for $n=1$. - ✅ **Pressure sensors** use a micromachined diaphragm; the maximum stress is $\sigma_{\max} = 0.308 P a^2 / h^2$ for a square plate. - ✅ **Capacitive pressure sensors** offer higher temperature stability than piezoresistive ones but require more complex readout electronics. - ✅ **Optical MEMS sensors** responsivity is $R = \eta q \lambda / h c$ in A/W. - ✅ In **microfluidics**, $Re \ll 1$ and surface tension dominates; flow is laminar and predictable. - ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille flow** governs pressure-driven flow in microchannels: $Q \propto h^3 w \Delta P / \mu L$. - ✅ The **Capillary number** $Ca$ quantifies the competition between viscous and surface-tension forces. - ✅ Always identify the **transduction principle** before describing any sensor in an exam — this is the KTU mark-winning opener. - ✅ Mention **selectivity, sensitivity, resolution, dynamic range, and response time** when comparing sensor technologies. - ✅ The most commercially successful MEMS sensors to date are: **pressure sensors** (automotive TPMS, medical), **MEMS microphones**, and **inkjet printheads** (microfluidics). - ✅ Real-world examples to cite in KTU answers: smartphone barometers, continuous glucose monitors (Dexcom), and automotive airbag accelerometers (if asked in cross-questions).

topics.title: Engineering Science for Microsystem Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT632 - MICRO-ELECTRO-MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Engineering Science for Microsystem Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Engineering Science for Microsystem Design

**Engineering Science for Microsystem Design** is the disciplined application of continuum mechanics, thermodynamics, and physical principles of materials at the micro and nano scale to predict, analyze, and control the behavior of Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS). It forms the analytical backbone that allows engineers to translate macroscopic laws (Hooke's law, Fourier's law, Navier-Stokes equations) into microscale design rules for thin films, beams, plates, and membranes fabricated using silicon, polysilicon, silicon dioxide, silicon nitride, polymers, and metals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module specifically demands competence in **Intrinsic Stress**, **Stress in Bulk Micromachining**, **Material Properties**, **Poisson's Ratio**, **Young's Modulus**, and **Thermodynamics (Heat Conduction & Thermal Expansion)** for thin-film and structural mechanics problems.

## 1.2 Fundamental Quantities — The Three Pillars

The entire analytical framework of microsystem design rests on three foundational pillars:

### Pillar 1: Mechanical Stress and Strain
A solid body subjected to a load develops **stress (σ)**, which is force per unit area, measured in **N/m² (Pa)**. The resulting deformation is **strain (ε)**, a dimensionless ratio of the deformed length to the original length.

### Pillar 2: Thermal Behaviour
Microsystems, due to their very low thermal mass, exhibit dramatic temperature variations during operation, packaging, and service. **Thermal expansion (α·ΔT)** and **heat conduction (q = -k·∇T)** must be quantified precisely.

### Pillar 3: Material Properties
At the microscale, grain size, surface-to-volume ratio, and fabrication-induced defects dictate the actual **Young's Modulus (E)**, **Poisson's Ratio (ν)**, density (ρ), and thermal conductivity (k), which often differ from bulk textbook values.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Inflated Balloon and the Steel Plate

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine you inflate a balloon and then press a thin steel plate against its curved surface. The balloon wants to expand (analogous to a thin film trying to contract due to **intrinsic stress**), but the steel plate resists this motion (analogous to the **substrate** in MEMS). The result is a biaxial stress field — the same phenomenon that causes thin deposited films to crack, peel, or buckle on a silicon wafer. The *engineering science* quantifies this tug-of-war mathematically.

A second powerful analogy: a **bimetallic strip** in an old thermostat. Two metals with different coefficients of thermal expansion bonded together bend when heated. A polysilicon–silicon dioxide bilayer in a MEMS thermal actuator does exactly the same thing, but with micrometer dimensions.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Young's Modulus of single-crystal silicon (in-plane):** **E = 190 GPa (along <110> direction in (100) wafer)**, **E = 130 GPa (along <100>)**
- **Poisson's Ratio of silicon:** **ν ≈ 0.28**
- **Density of silicon:** **ρ = 2330 kg/m³**
- **Thermal conductivity of silicon:** **k ≈ 150 W/(m·K)**
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion of silicon:** **α ≈ 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K**
- **Intrinsic stress in as-deposited LPCVD polysilicon:** **σ ≈ 300–500 MPa (tensile)**
- **Thermal conductivity of silicon dioxide:** **k ≈ 1.4 W/(m·K)**
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion of SiO₂:** **α ≈ 0.5 × 10⁻⁶ /K**

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Real Microsystems

Without proper engineering-science analysis:
- **Thin-film diaphragms** in pressure sensors will crack due to uncontrolled intrinsic stress.
- **Comb-drive actuators** will fail to provide rated displacement if residual stress is ignored.
- **Bolometers and IR sensors** will have unstable output due to thermal mismatch.
- **Gyroscopes and accelerometers** will exhibit thermal drift.
- **Micro-heaters** will fail due to localised thermal stress concentration.

In short: **engineering science = design reliability.** It is the difference between a functional microsystem and a fabricated artifact that fails under vacuum, thermal cycling, or mechanical shock.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stress — A Hierarchical Classification

Stress in a microsystem is rarely a single quantity. It is a **tensor** (in rigorous 3-D analysis) with multiple physical origins:

1. **Intrinsic Stress (σᵢ):** Built into the film during deposition due to grain-boundary relaxation, impurity incorporation, or phase transformation. It exists *even when no external load is applied.*
2. **Residual Stress (σᵣ):** The stress remaining after the fabrication process (etching, release, dicing). Combination of intrinsic + thermal.
3. **Thermal Stress (σ_T):** Generated due to mismatch in coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) when temperature changes from fabrication temperature to operating temperature.
4. **Applied / External Stress (σ_a):** From packaging, shock, vibration, fluidic pressure, or inertial loading.
5. **Stress Gradient:** A through-thickness variation of stress, which causes released structures to bend (curl).

The **total in-plane stress** acting on a thin film bonded to a substrate is therefore:

$$
\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_i + \sigma_T = \sigma_i + \frac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}\,\alpha_f (T_{\text{dep}} - T_{\text{op}})
$$

where the subscript *f* denotes the film and *dep* denotes deposition temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoney's Theorem** (1909) — A landmark result: the average stress in a thin film deposited on a thick substrate can be obtained purely from the **substrate curvature** measurement.

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 \, t_f \, R}
$$

where **E_s/(1-ν_s)** is the biaxial modulus of the substrate, *t_s* and *t_f* are thicknesses of substrate and film, and *R* is the radius of curvature induced by the stressed film.

## 2.2 Stress vs. Strain — Hooke's Law and Beyond

For a 1-D elastic body, Hooke's law gives:

$$
\sigma = E \, \varepsilon
$$

For a 2-D thin film constrained by a substrate (plane-stress condition), the biaxial modulus governs:

$$
\sigma = \frac{E}{1-\nu}\,\varepsilon
$$

When a film is **constrained** (cannot contract laterally because of the substrate), the in-plane strain is forced to equal the substrate strain:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_{\text{substrate}}
$$

The corresponding in-plane stress components are:

$$
\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \frac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}\,\varepsilon_{\text{film}}
$$

## 2.3 Biaxial Modulus — Why We Need It

For a thin film bonded to a substrate, the in-plane stress is equibiaxial. Hooke's law in 2-D plane-stress is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \end{bmatrix}
=
\frac{E}{1-\nu^2}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu \\ \nu & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \end{bmatrix}
$$

The combined constant **E/(1-ν)** is the **biaxial modulus**, denoted **Y**:

$$
Y = \frac{E}{1-\nu}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The biaxial modulus *Y* is the single most important material constant used to estimate thin-film stresses in MEMS. For silicon with E = 190 GPa and ν = 0.28, **Y ≈ 264 GPa**.

## 2.4 Poisson's Ratio — The Lateral Effect

When a rod is stretched longitudinally, it contracts laterally. The ratio of lateral to axial strain is the Poisson's ratio:

$$
\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}
$$

A typical range is **0 < ν < 0.5** for most engineering materials. **Incompressible materials (rubber) approach ν = 0.5.** For silicon, ν ≈ 0.28. For soft polymers used in flexible MEMS (PDMS, SU-8), ν ≈ 0.45–0.49.

## 2.5 Thermal Effects — Two Coupled Phenomena

### A. Thermal Expansion
A free body of length L expands upon heating:

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \, L_0 \, \Delta T
$$

The associated strain is:

$$
\varepsilon_T = \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

### B. Thermal Stress in a Constrained Film
If the film is *constrained*, the thermal strain produces a stress:

$$
\sigma_T = Y \, \alpha_f \, \Delta T = \frac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}\,\alpha_f \, \Delta T
$$

This is the **dominant failure mechanism** in multilayer MEMS stacks.

### C. Heat Conduction (Fourier's Law)
Steady-state 1-D conduction:

$$
q_x = -k \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

Where **q_x** is the heat flux (W/m²), **k** is the thermal conductivity, and **dT/dx** is the temperature gradient.

For a slab of thickness *t*:

$$
q = \frac{k}{t}\,(T_1 - T_2)
$$

For **transient conduction**, the lumped-capacitance heat-transfer equation:

$$
\rho \, V \, c_p \,\frac{dT}{dt} = q_{\text{in}} - q_{\text{out}}
$$

### D. Thermal Time Constant
A critical design parameter for thermal MEMS (bolometers, micro-calorimeters):

$$
\tau = \frac{\rho \, c_p \, V}{h \, A}
$$

where *h* is the convective heat-transfer coefficient, *A* is surface area, and *V* is volume.

## 2.6 Stress in Bulk Micromachining — Anisotropy of Silicon

Single-crystal silicon exhibits **anisotropic etching**. The etch rate depends strongly on crystallographic orientation. KOH etches silicon **~400× faster in the <100> direction than in the <111> direction.**

This anisotropy produces well-defined V-grooves bounded by {111} planes, with sidewall angles of **54.74°** with respect to the wafer surface (for (100) wafers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Young's Modulus is also anisotropic in silicon.** The effective modulus in different directions of a (100) wafer:
> - <100> direction: E = 130 GPa
> - <110> direction: E = 190 GPa
> - <111> direction: E = 188 GPa
>
> Always choose the modulus along the *load direction*, not the wafer normal.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | σ = E·ε | Hooke's Law (1-D) | Pa |
| 2 | Y = E/(1-ν) | Biaxial Modulus | Pa |
| 3 | σ_film = Y_f · ε_film | Plane-Stress Stress | Pa |
| 4 | ε_T = α·ΔT | Thermal Strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | σ_T = Y_f · α_f · ΔT | Thermal Stress | Pa |
| 6 | σ_total = σ_i + σ_T | Total In-Plane Stress | Pa |
| 7 | σ_f = (E_s · t_s²) / (6 · t_f · R) | Stoney's Formula | Pa |
| 8 | ν = -ε_lateral / ε_axial | Poisson's Ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | q_x = -k · dT/dx | Fourier's Law (1-D) | W/m² |
| 10 | q = (k/t)·(T₁ - T₂) | Conduction through slab | W/m² |
| 11 | τ = ρ·c_p·V/(h·A) | Thermal Time Constant | s |
| 12 | α = ΔL/(L₀·ΔT) | CTE Definition | 1/K |
| 13 | κ = k/(ρ·c_p) | Thermal Diffusivity | m²/s |
| 14 | Δθ = 54.74° | KOH V-groove wall angle (100-Si) | degrees |
| 15 | R_(100)/R_(111) ≈ 400 | KOH selectivity | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** For KTU questions, the *Biaxial Modulus Y = E/(1-ν)* form is almost always used for thin-film problems — not the plain E. Writing the wrong denominator costs 1–2 marks per question.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure sensors:** Diaphragm deflection under pressure is governed by plate theory + residual stress.
- **Inertial sensors (accelerometers/gyros):** Proof-mass displacement depends on spring stiffness (Y·I/L³) and damping (squeeze-film).
- **Microfluidic chips:** Capillary pressure scales with 1/r, but thermal expansion governs valve actuation.
- **Optical MEMS (DMD, MOEMS):** Mirror flatness depends critically on stress gradient.
- **RF MEMS switches:** Creep and fatigue in metallic beams are governed by σ, ν, and temperature.
- **Bio-MEMS:** Polymer modulus and CTE mismatch are central to organ-on-chip reliability.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stoney's Formula (Thin-Film Stress from Substrate Curvature)

**Given:**
- A thin film of thickness *t_f* and biaxial modulus *Y_f = E_f/(1-ν_f)* is bonded to a thick substrate of thickness *t_s* and biaxial modulus *Y_s*.
- The film is under uniform biaxial stress σ_f.
- The substrate is forced to bend to a radius *R* to maintain compatibility.

**Assumptions (Stoney's Original):**
1. Film is thin compared to substrate: t_f ≪ t_s.
2. Both film and substrate are homogeneous and isotropic.
3. Substrate bending is small (R ≫ t_s).
4. Film stress is uniform and equibiaxial.

**Step 1 — Force Equivalence:**
The film exerts a force per unit length on the substrate equal to:

$$
F = \sigma_f \, t_f
$$

This force is balanced by the bending moment in the substrate:

$$
M = \frac{Y_s \, t_s^3}{12} \cdot \frac{1}{R}
$$

(The standard bending moment-curvature relation: M = EI/ρ, where I = t_s³/12.)

**Step 2 — Moment Balance:**
The thin film, located at distance (t_s/2 + t_f/2) ≈ t_s/2 from the neutral axis (since t_f ≪ t_s), produces a moment:

$$
M = F \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Equating the Two Expressions:**

$$
\sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \frac{Y_s \, t_s^3}{12 R}
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for σ_f:**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{Y_s \, t_s^2}{6 \, t_f \, R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoney's Formula (Final):** σ_f = Y_s · t_s² / (6 · t_f · R)
> 
> This is the single most important equation for *measuring* thin-film stress using a wafer-curvature tool (like a Tencor FLX or a KLA-Tencor stylus profilometer).

## 3.2 Derivation of Thermal Stress in a Constrained Thin Film

**Given:** A thin film of thickness *t_f* and biaxial modulus *Y_f* is rigidly bonded to a substrate. The system is at deposition temperature *T_dep* (assumed stress-free) and is cooled to operating temperature *T_op*.

**Step 1 — Free Thermal Strain (Unconstrained):**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{free}} = \alpha_f \, (T_{\text{dep}} - T_{\text{op}}) = \alpha_f \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Constrained Strain (Bonded to Substrate):**
Because the film cannot contract independently, the substrate dictates the actual strain:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{actual}} = \alpha_s \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 3 — Elastic Strain (the Mismatch):**
The difference between what the film "wants" and what it "actually experiences" is the *elastic strain* responsible for stress:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{elastic}} = \varepsilon_{\text{free}} - \varepsilon_{\text{actual}} = (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 4 — Hooke's Law (Biaxial Plane Stress):**

$$
\sigma_T = Y_f \, \varepsilon_{\text{elastic}} = \frac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}\,(\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \, \Delta T
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** σ_T = [E_f / (1-ν_f)] · (α_f - α_s) · ΔT
> 
> The sign of σ_T depends on the relative magnitudes of α_f and α_s. If α_f > α_s and the system is cooled (ΔT > 0), the film wants to contract more than the substrate → film is in **tensile** stress. This is exactly the cause of cracking in thick PECVD oxide films on silicon.

## 3.3 Derivation of Biaxial Stress from Hooke's Law in 2-D

**Given:** An isotropic thin film under plane stress (σ_z = 0), subjected to in-plane strains ε_x and ε_y.

**Constitutive Relation (Generalized Hooke's Law):**

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\,(\varepsilon_x + \nu\,\varepsilon_y)
$$

$$
\sigma_y = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\,(\varepsilon_y + \nu\,\varepsilon_x)
$$

**Step 1 — Special Case: Equibiaxial Strain (ε_x = ε_y = ε):**

$$
\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\,(1 + \nu)\,\varepsilon = \frac{E}{1-\nu}\,\varepsilon
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Biaxial Modulus:**

$$
Y = \frac{E}{1-\nu}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Expression:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{biaxial}} = Y \cdot \varepsilon
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The factor (1-ν) in the denominator arises because in 2-D constraint, the Poisson contraction in one direction is *suppressed* by the in-plane stress in the perpendicular direction. Hence the material appears "stiffer" in 2-D than in 1-D by a factor of 1/(1-ν) ≈ 1.4 for silicon.

## 3.4 Derivation of Thermal Time Constant for a Micro-Heater

**Given:** A suspended micro-bridge of length *L*, width *w*, and thickness *t*, made of polysilicon, surrounded by air. Power *P* is dissipated electrically. The bridge reaches steady-state temperature *T_ss* after a transient.

**Step 1 — Energy Balance (Lumped Model):**

$$
\rho \, V \, c_p \,\frac{dT}{dt} = P_{\text{in}} - h A (T - T_0)
$$

where *V = L·w·t*, *A = 2·L·w + 2·L·t + 2·w·t* (total surface area, all six faces).

**Step 2 — Define Excess Temperature θ = T - T₀:**

$$
\rho \, V \, c_p \,\frac{d\theta}{dt} + h A \, \theta = P_{\text{in}}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard First-Order ODE Solution:**

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_{ss}\,\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)
$$

with steady-state excess temperature:

$$
\theta_{ss} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{h A}
$$

and **thermal time constant:**

$$
\tau = \frac{\rho \, c_p \, V}{h \, A}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** τ = ρ·c_p·V/(h·A)
> 
> For a typical 100 µm × 100 µm × 2 µm polysilicon micro-hotplate in air (h ≈ 100 W/m²·K, ρ·c_p ≈ 1.66 × 10⁶ J/m³·K), the time constant is on the order of **1–10 ms**. This is why micro-hotplate gas sensors can be pulsed at 10–100 Hz.

## 3.5 Derivation of Stress Distribution in a Suspended Beam (Pre-Stress Effect)

**Given:** A doubly-clamped beam of length *L*, thickness *t*, width *w*, and built-in axial pre-stress *σ₀*, under a transverse point load *F* at its centre.

**Step 1 — Total Stiffness (Bending + Stretching):**

$$
k_{\text{total}} = k_{\text{bending}} + k_{\text{axial}}
$$

**Step 2 — Bending Stiffness:**

$$
k_{\text{bending}} = \frac{192 \, E I}{L^3}, \quad I = \frac{w t^3}{12}
$$

(For a doubly-clamped beam with central point load, the classical Euler-Bernoulli formula.)

**Step 3 — Axial Stiffness from Pre-Stress:**
The pre-tension acts like a string stiffness:

$$
k_{\text{axial}} = \frac{4 \, \sigma_0 \, w \, t}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Central Deflection Under Load F:**

$$
d = \frac{F}{k_{\text{total}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** If the pre-stress σ₀ is compressive (negative), then k_axial is *negative*, and at a critical value known as the **buckling load**, the beam becomes unstable. This is the principle behind stress-engineered buckled-beam sensors (compressively pre-stressed thin films).

## 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Engineering Situation | Governing Equation | Key Assumption |
|----------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Measuring film stress via wafer curvature | Stoney's Formula | t_f ≪ t_s |
| Stress in cooled film on substrate | σ_T = Y·(α_f - α_s)·ΔT | Rigid substrate, equibiaxial |
| Diaphragm deflection under pressure | Plate equation with residual stress | Plate theory valid |
| Micro-heater response time | τ = ρ·c_p·V/(h·A) | Lumped thermal mass |
| Heat loss to anchor | 1-D fin equation | θ(anchor) = T₀ |
| Pull-in voltage of comb drive | V_PI = sqrt(8·k·d³/(27·ε₀·A)) | Small deflection, no fringing |
| Buckling of compressive beam | P_cr = 4·π²·E·I/L² | Euler column theory |
| Thermal expansion of free body | ΔL = α·L₀·ΔT | Unconstrained |
