<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three important silicon compounds used in MEMS fabrication and mention one specific application for each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)** — Used as a sacrificial layer in surface micromachining and as a dielectric in capacitive sensors.
2. **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)** — Used as an etch mask during anisotropic wet etching of silicon in KOH, due to its extremely low etch rate.
3. **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** — Used as the structural material in high-temperature MEMS sensors operating above 600 °C, such as jet engine pressure transducers. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between dry and wet thermal oxidation of silicon.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Dry Oxidation | Wet Oxidation |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Reactant | O₂ | H₂O vapour |
| Reaction | Si + O₂ → SiO₂ | Si + 2H₂O → SiO₂ + 2H₂ |
| Growth rate | Slower (~0.04 μm/hr) | Faster (~0.7 μm/hr) |
| Oxide quality | Dense, dry, pinhole-free | Softer, porous |
| Use case | Gate dielectric, mask | Thick sacrificial / field oxide | (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - In Deal-Grove numerical problems, students **forget to set $x_i = 0$** and **lose 2 marks**.
> - Students often **omit units** (μm²/hr) — board examiner deducts **0.5 mark per equation**.
> - Confusing **B** (parabolic) with **B/A** (linear) is the **#1 mistake** in MEMS theory papers. Memorise: B → parabolic; B/A → linear.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the Deal-Grove model for thermal oxidation of silicon and explain the two limiting regimes with physical reasoning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon wafer is dry-oxidized at 1100 °C for 3 hours. Given B = 0.09 μm²/hr and A = 0.15 μm, and initial oxide = 0.05 μm, calculate the final oxide thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Derivation

[Present the full Deal-Grove derivation exactly as in Section 3.1, Steps 1 to 7, with each of the three fluxes F₁, F₂, F₃ defined and the steady-state continuity condition applied. Conclude with the two regimes.]

**Physical Reasoning of Regimes:**

- **Linear regime (thin oxide, $x_0 \ll A$):** The oxide is so thin that diffusion through it is **instantaneous** compared to the chemical reaction at the Si interface. The **surface reaction** is the **rate-limiting step** → linear in time. (≈ 3.5 marks)

- **Parabolic regime (thick oxide, $x_0 \gg A$):** The oxide is so thick that diffusion of oxidant through it is **very slow** and becomes the **rate-limiting step** → quadratic in time. (≈ 3.5 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical

**Step 1 — Write the Deal-Grove equation:**

$$x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$$

**Step 2 — Compute τ with $x_i = 0.05 \,\mu m$:**

$$\tau = \frac{x_i^2 + A x_i}{B} = \frac{(0.05)^2 + (0.15)(0.05)}{0.09}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{0.0025 + 0.0075}{0.09} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.0100}{0.09} \\[4pt]
&= 0.111\,\text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute t = 3 hr:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_0^2 + 0.15 x_0 &= 0.09 \,(3 + 0.111) \\
x_0^2 + 0.15 x_0 &= 0.09 \times 3.111 \\
x_0^2 + 0.15 x_0 &= 0.280
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve quadratic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_0 &= \frac{-0.15 + \sqrt{0.0225 + 1.120}}{2} \\
&= \frac{-0.15 + \sqrt{1.1425}}{2} \\
&= \frac{-0.15 + 1.0689}{2} \\
&= 0.459\,\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $x_0 \approx 0.46 \,\mu m$ **(7 marks — full credit)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the role of SiO₂ as a sacrificial layer in surface micromachining. Compare PSG, BPSG, and undoped SiO₂ as sacrificial materials. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LPCVD Si₃N₄ cantilever of length 200 μm and width 30 μm, deposited on SiO₂, has a residual tensile stress of 800 MPa. After HF release, calculate the maximum deflection. Given: E_SiN = 250 GPa, thickness = 1 μm. State whether the cantilever will buckle or remain flat. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Sacrificial Layer Concept

**Process flow (use the Mermaid block of Section 4.2 as the basis):**

1. Start with **Si substrate**.
2. **Deposit** sacrificial SiO₂ (~2 μm) by LPCVD.
3. **Pattern** anchor openings using photolithography + BHF etch.
4. **Deposit** structural Si₃N₄ (~1 μm) by LPCVD.
5. **Pattern** the nitride using RIE.
6. **Release** in **buffered HF (BHF)** — SiO₂ dissolves, freeing the cantilever.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Undoped SiO₂ | PSG (8% P) | BPSG (4% B, 4% P) |
|----------|--------------|------------|--------------------|
| Etch rate in BHF | Moderate (~80 nm/min) | High (~500 nm/min) | Very high (~1000 nm/min) |
| Reflow temperature | None | ~950 °C | ~850 °C |
| Planarization | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Released time | Long | Medium | Short |

(7 marks — 4 marks process + 3 marks comparison)

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Stress & Buckling

**Step 1 — Identify if stress is tensile or compressive.**

The given stress is **tensile (800 MPa > 0)**. Tensile stress **does not cause buckling**; only **compressive** stress does.

**Step 2 — Compute axial force on cantilever:**

$$F = \sigma \cdot A = \sigma \cdot w \cdot t$$

$$F = 800 \times 10^6 \times (30 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) = 24 \times 10^{-3}\,N = 24\,mN$$

**Step 3 — Maximum deflection at the tip:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{F L^3}{3 E I} \\[4pt]
I &= \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(30\times 10^{-6})(1\times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-19}\,m^4 \\[4pt]
\delta &= \frac{(24\times 10^{-3})(200\times 10^{-6})^3}{3 \times (250\times 10^9) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-19})} \\[4pt]
\delta &= \frac{24 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-12}}{1.875 \times 10^{-7}} \\[4pt]
\delta &= \frac{1.92 \times 10^{-13}}{1.875 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.024 \times 10^{-6}\,m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\delta \approx 1.02\,\mu m$ (negligible). Since the stress is **tensile**, the cantilever **will NOT buckle**; it remains essentially flat with a 1 μm elongation. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision — read this in 90 seconds before the exam.**

- **SiO₂** — grown thermally (Deal-Grove), sacrificial layer, etch mask, dielectric.
- **Si₃N₄** — LPCVD/PECVD, etch stop in KOH, structural layer, high modulus.
- **SiC** — high-temperature MEMS (>600 °C), chemical inertness.
- **PSG / BPSG** — doped glasses for fast sacrificial release and planarization.
- **Silicides (TiSi₂, CoSi₂)** — low-resistivity interconnects.
- **Pyrex 7740** — anodic bonding partner (matched CTE).
- **Deal-Grove Equation:** $x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$ — always state units.
- **Wet oxidation ≈ 10× faster than dry**, but lower quality.
- **Etch selectivity** Si:SiO₂ in KOH ≈ 400:1; Si:Si₃N₄ ≈ 10000:1.
- **Stoney formula:** $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s t_s^2}{6 t_f R}$ for film stress measurement.
- **Tensile stress** → no buckling. **Compressive stress** → buckling risk.
- **Anodic bonding:** Si to Pyrex at ~300–400 °C, 600–1000 V.
- For numericals: always convert minutes → hours when using B (in μm²/hr).
- Always mention the **two regimes** (linear + parabolic) for full marks in theory questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silicon Piezoresistors, Gallium Arsenide
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT632 - MICRO-ELECTRO-MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Silicon Piezoresistors, Gallium Arsenide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Silicon Piezoresistors

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
A **Silicon Piezoresistor** is a doped silicon resistive element in which the electrical resistivity changes in a well-defined, anisotropic manner when subjected to mechanical stress. The phenomenon, known as the **Piezoresistive Effect**, was first observed by Lord Kelvin in 1856 and later quantitatively developed by C.S. Smith (1954) for germanium and silicon. In semiconductors like silicon, the piezoresistive effect is **two orders of magnitude larger** than the corresponding effect in metallic strain gauges, making it the dominant sensing mechanism in modern MEMS pressure sensors and inertial sensors.

The fundamental governing relation is given by:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_L \, \sigma_l + \pi_T \, \sigma_t$$

where $\pi_L$ and $\pi_T$ are the **longitudinal** and **transverse piezoresistive coefficients** (units: $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ or $\text{m}^2/\text{N}$), and $\sigma_l$, $\sigma_t$ are the corresponding stress components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** For KTU examinations, always remember that the piezoresistive effect in silicon is primarily due to **changes in the band structure (mobility and carrier concentration)**, not geometric deformation. This is the opposite of metallic strain gauges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Silicon piezoresistors form the sensing backbone of piezoresistive pressure sensors (e.g., the Motorola/Freescale MPX series), accelerometers, and flow sensors studied later in this module.

### 1.2 Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) as a MEMS Material

**Formal Definition:**
**Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)** is a III-V direct-bandgap compound semiconductor with a zinc-blende crystal structure, an energy bandgap of **1.42 eV** at 300 K, and electron mobility approximately **6 times higher** than silicon. In the context of MEMS, GaAs is significant for two reasons: (1) it exhibits the piezoresistive effect (used in high-temperature sensors), and (2) it is inherently **piezoelectric**, enabling piezoelectric transduction in addition to piezoresistive sensing.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stressed Rubber Band"

Imagine a **rubber band** with a printed pattern of conductive ink lines. When you stretch the band:
- The lines get **longer** and **thinner** — this is the *geometric* resistance change (small effect).
- The electrical *conductivity* of the ink itself changes because the molecular structure distorts — this is the *piezoresistive* effect (large effect in silicon, small in metals).

In **silicon**, the second effect dominates. In **GaAs**, both the piezoresistive and piezoelectric effects coexist, giving it a "dual personality" in sensor design.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Silicon (Si) | Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) |
|---|---|---|
| Crystal Structure | Diamond (Cubic) | Zinc-Blende (Cubic) |
| Bandgap @ 300 K | **1.12 eV** (indirect) | **1.42 eV** (direct) |
| Electron Mobility $\mu_n$ | **1400 cm²/V·s** | **8500 cm²/V·s** |
| Hole Mobility $\mu_p$ | **450 cm²/V·s** | **400 cm²/V·s** |
| Density | **2.33 g/cm³** | **5.32 g/cm³** |
| Young's Modulus | **130 GPa (⟨100⟩)** | **85 GPa (⟨100⟩)** |
| Gauge Factor (typical) | **50–200** | **−5 to −50** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Piezoresistive Effect — Underlying Physics

The total fractional change in resistance of a resistor under stress has two contributions:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \underbrace{(1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon}_{\text{Geometric term}} + \underbrace{\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}}_{\text{Piezoresistive term}}$$

- **Geometric term** $(1+2\nu)\varepsilon$: arises from change in length and cross-section. For Si, Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.28$, so this term is small ($\approx 1.6\varepsilon$).
- **Piezoresistive term** $\Delta \rho / \rho$: arises from stress-induced changes in the silicon energy band structure, affecting carrier mobility. This is the **dominant term** in semiconductors.

The **Gauge Factor (G)** is defined as the sensitivity of resistance change to strain:

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\pi_l \, E}{1} \quad (\text{approximate form for uniaxial stress})$$

For doped silicon at typical doping levels ($10^{18}$ to $10^{20}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$), $G \approx 50$ to $200$, while for metals $G \approx 2$.

### 2.2 The Three Independent Piezoresistive Coefficients

Because silicon has cubic (m3m) crystal symmetry, the piezoresistive tensor has only **three independent components**:

| Coefficient | Typical Value (p-type Si, 10 Ω·cm) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\pi_{11}$ | $\approx +6.6 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Stress parallel to current in $\langle 100 \rangle$ direction |
| $\pi_{12}$ | $\approx -1.1 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Stress perpendicular to current in $\langle 100 \rangle$ |
| $\pi_{44}$ | $\approx +138.1 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Shear piezoresistive coefficient (dominant in p-Si) |

For n-type Si, $\pi_{44}$ is the dominant coefficient (about $-136 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$), but the *orientation* of maximum sensitivity differs.

### 2.3 Longitudinal and Transverse Coefficients in Arbitrary Direction

For a resistor aligned along direction $\vec{m} = (m_1, m_2, m_3)$ with current flow, and stress applied along direction $\vec{l} = (l_1, l_2, l_3)$, the directional piezoresistive coefficients are:

$$\pi_l = \pi_{11} - 2\left(\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \frac{\pi_{44}}{2}\right)\left(l_1^2 m_1^2 + l_2^2 m_2^2 + l_3^2 m_3^2\right)$$

$$\pi_t = \pi_{12} + \left(\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \frac{\pi_{44}}{2}\right)\left(l_1^2 m_1^2 + l_2^2 m_2^2 + l_3^2 m_3^2\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "What orientation gives maximum piezoresistance?", the answer depends on doping type:
> - **p-type Si:** $\langle 111 \rangle$ direction (along body diagonal of cube)
> - **n-type Si:** $\langle 100 \rangle$ direction (along cube edge)
>
> This is a frequently asked 3-mark question!

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_L \sigma_l + \pi_T \sigma_t$ | Full piezoresistive response | Pressure sensor output |
| $\pi_l = \pi_{11} - 2(\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \pi_{44}/2)F$ | Longitudinal coefficient | Orientation design |
| $\pi_t = \pi_{12} + (\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \pi_{44}/2)F$ | Transverse coefficient | Orientation design |
| $F = l_1^2 m_1^2 + l_2^2 m_2^2 + l_3^2 m_3^2$ | Orientation factor | Crystal direction analysis |
| $G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$ | Gauge Factor (semiconductor) | Sensitivity calculation |
| $\Delta V / V = \pi_{44}/2 \cdot (\sigma_l - \sigma_t)$ | Wheatstone bridge output | Sensor readout |
| $V_{\text{out}} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} \approx \dfrac{V_s}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Half-bridge output | Final signal |

### 2.5 Doping and Temperature Dependence

- **Doping Concentration:** Piezoresistive coefficients decrease with increasing doping. The empirical relation is often written as:
$$\pi(N) \approx \frac{\pi_0}{1 + (N/N_{\text{ref}})^{p}}$$
where $N$ is doping concentration, $N_{\text{ref}} \approx 10^{18}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$, and $p \approx 0.5$ to $1$.

- **Temperature:** Piezoresistive coefficients generally *increase* with temperature up to about 100 °C, then decrease. This is the **Temperature Coefficient of Piezoresistance (TCP)**, typically **−0.1 %/°C to −0.3 %/°C** for moderate doping.

### 2.6 Gallium Arsenide — Engineering Relevance

| Property | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| Wide bandgap (1.42 eV) | Operation up to **350–400 °C** (vs ~150 °C for Si) |
| Direct bandgap | Integrated optical MEMS / opto-MEMS |
| High electron mobility | Faster piezoresistive response, RF MEMS |
| Piezoelectric (zinc-blende symmetry) | Dual piezoresistive + piezoelectric sensing |
| Semi-insulating substrate | Reduced parasitic capacitance |

GaAs is therefore preferred for **harsh-environment pressure sensors** (aerospace, oil/gas downhole), **opto-MEMS**, and **RF MEMS** where silicon's lower mobility and temperature limit become bottlenecks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Strain to Resistance Change

**Starting point:** The resistance of a uniform resistor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ is:
$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 1 — Take the total differential** (logarithmic differentiation) to express fractional change:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Express geometric terms in terms of axial strain** $\varepsilon = dL/L$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$:
$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$
The negative sign appears because area contracts when length extends (for $\nu > 0$).

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

This is the **master equation** separating geometric and piezoresistive contributions.

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's law for uniaxial stress** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = (1 + 2\nu)\frac{\sigma}{E} + \pi_l \sigma$$

**Step 5 — Define the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient** as the proportionality between $\Delta\rho/\rho$ and axial stress:
$$\pi_l = \frac{1}{E}\frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}\bigg|_{\text{uniaxial}}$$

**Step 6 — Define the gauge factor** $G = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$:
$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$$

**Numerical verification for p-type Si (light doping):**
With $\nu = 0.28$, $\pi_l = 100 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$, $E = 130 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
G &= 1 + 2(0.28) + (100 \times 10^{-11})(130 \times 10^9) \\
&= 1 + 0.56 + 130 \\
&= 131.56
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the experimentally observed gauge factor of **~100 to 200** for p-type silicon.

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Piezoresistance Direction for p-type Si

**Given:** For p-type silicon, $\pi_{44} \gg \pi_{11}, \pi_{12}$ (shear dominates). We want to find the direction $\vec{m} = (m_1, m_2, m_3)$ that maximizes $\pi_l$ when stress is applied along the same direction (i.e., $\vec{l} = \vec{m}$).

**Step 1 — Set $\vec{l} = \vec{m}$**, so $l_i = m_i$. The orientation factor becomes:
$$F = m_1^4 + m_2^4 + m_3^4$$

**Step 2 — Constraint:** The direction cosines satisfy the normalization condition:
$$m_1^2 + m_2^2 + m_3^2 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Maximize** $F$ subject to the constraint using Lagrange multipliers, or by symmetry argument. By symmetry, the maximum occurs when all three components are equal:
$$m_1 = m_2 = m_3 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the value of $F$ at this point:**
$$F_{\max} = 3 \times \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^4 = 3 \times \frac{1}{9} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the longitudinal coefficient formula:**
$$\pi_l^{\max} = \pi_{11} - 2\left(\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \frac{\pi_{44}}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{\pi_{11} + 2\pi_{12} + \pi_{44}}{3}$$

For p-type Si with $\pi_{11} \approx 6.6$, $\pi_{12} \approx -1.1$, $\pi_{44} \approx 138.1$ (all $\times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi_l^{\max} &= \frac{6.6 + 2(-1.1) + 138.1}{3} \times 10^{-11} \\
&= \frac{6.6 - 2.2 + 138.1}{3} \times 10^{-11} \\
&= \frac{142.5}{3} \times 10^{-11} \\
&\approx 47.5 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

The direction $(1/\sqrt{3}, 1/\sqrt{3}, 1/\sqrt{3})$ corresponds to the **⟨111⟩** family of crystal directions.

### 3.3 Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Output for a Piezoresistive Sensor

**Setup:** A piezoresistive pressure sensor uses a fully active Wheatstone bridge with four identical piezoresistors, two oriented longitudinally and two transversely relative to the maximum stress direction.

**Step 1 — Resistance changes under stress:**
- Longitudinal resistors: $R_1 = R_3 = R(1 + \pi_l \sigma_l)$
- Transverse resistors: $R_2 = R_4 = R(1 + \pi_t \sigma_t)$

**Step 2 — Output voltage of bridge** (with supply $V_s$):
$$V_{\text{out}} = V_s \left[ \frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} - \frac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify** (assuming $|\Delta R/R| \ll 1$):
$$V_{\text{out}} \approx \frac{V_s}{4}\left(\frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the piezoresistive relations:**
$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_s}{2}\left(\pi_l \sigma_l - \pi_t \sigma_t\right)$$

**Step 5 — For a diaphragm under differential pressure** with $\sigma_t = -\nu\sigma_l$ (using the biaxial stress state):
$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_s}{2}\,\pi_l\,\sigma_l\left(1 + \nu\frac{\pi_t}{\pi_l}\right)$$

**Numerical illustration:** Let $V_s = 5\,\text{V}$, $\pi_l = 50 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$, $\pi_t = -20 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$, $\nu = 0.28$, $\sigma_l = 1 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}$ (1 MPa):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}} &= \frac{5}{2}(50 \times 10^{-11})(10^6)\left(1 + 0.28 \times \frac{-20}{50}\right) \\
&= 2.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.112) \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.888 \\
&\approx 1.11\,\text{mV}
\end{aligned}$$

This milli-volt range signal is typical of unamplified MEMS pressure sensor outputs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Applied Pressure P] --> B[Silicon Diaphragm]
    B --> C{Stress Distribution sigma_x, sigma_y}
    C --> D[Piezoresistor R1 along x at edge]
    C --> E[Piezoresistor R2 along y at edge]
    C --> F[Piezoresistor R3 along x at edge]
    C --> G[Piezoresistor R4 along y at edge]
    D --> H[Wheatstone Bridge Network]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Differential Output V_out]
    I --> J[Amplifier and Signal Conditioning]
    J --> K[Digital Output in mV or Counts]
    Dnote1[Doping: p-type, 10^18 cm^-3] -.-> D
    Dnote2[Orientation: <111> for max sensitivity] -.-> D
```

### 4.2 Crystal Orientation Map for Piezoresistor Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Silicon Wafer 100 surface] --> B{P-type Si: max pi along 111}
    A --> C{n-type Si: max pi along 100}
    B --> D[Diagonal Resistor Placement at 54.7 deg to wafer edge]
    C --> E[Edge-Aligned Resistor along Wafer Flat]
    D --> F[Implant p+ regions, define by lithography]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Metallize contacts, passivate]
    G --> H[Pressure Sensor Die]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: GaAs vs Si Piezoresistive System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENV [Operating Environment]
        E1[Room Temp to 150 C: Si Sensor Used]
        E2[Room Temp to 400 C: GaAs Sensor Used]
        E3[RF / Opto Integration: GaAs Preferred]
    end
    ENV --> SENSE
    subgraph SENSE [Sensing Element Choice]
        S1[Si: High GF, low noise, mature fab]
        S2[GaAs: Lower GF, high temp, optical, piezoelectric option]
    end
    SENSE --> READ
    subgraph READ [Readout Circuitry]
        R1[Wheatstone Bridge]
        R2[Amplifier and ADC]
        R3[Temp Compensation Network]
    end
    READ --> OUT
    subgraph OUT [Output]
        O1[Analog mV Signal]
        O2[Digital Calibrated Output]
    end
```

### 4.4 Piezoresistor Fabrication Cross-Section (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Bulk Si Wafer 100] --> B[Thermal Oxidation SiO2]
    B --> C[Boron Ion Implant for p-type Resistor]
    C --> D[Anneal and Activate Doping]
    D --> E[Deposit and Pattern Aluminum Contacts]
    E --> F[Passivation with Si3N4 or SiO2]
    F --> G[Anisotropic Etch in KOH to Form Diaphragm]
    G --> H[Anodic Bond to Glass Pyrex 7740]
    H --> I[Complete Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `sense1`) and labels are plain uppercase text without inline markdown formatting, in compliance with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the piezoresistive effect. Why is the piezoresistive effect in silicon significantly larger than in metals?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The piezoresistive effect is the change in electrical resistivity of a material when subjected to mechanical stress, quantified as $\Delta R / R = (1+2\nu)\varepsilon + \Delta \rho / \rho$. In metals, the effect is small (gauge factor ≈ 2) because conduction is by nearly-free electrons in a wide band, so stress perturbs the band only weakly. In silicon, conduction depends on the anisotropic, multi-valley band structure (or the heavy-hole/light-hole valence band in p-type). Stress redistributes carriers among valleys or bands, changing effective mass and mobility, giving gauge factors of 50–200. Hence the piezoresistive term dominates the geometric term. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the crystal directions along which maximum piezoresistance is observed in (a) p-type silicon, and (b) n-type silicon. Justify briefly.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
(a) **p-type Si:** Maximum piezoresistance is along the **⟨111⟩ direction** (body diagonal), because for p-type Si the shear piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_{44}$ dominates and the orientation factor $F = m_1^2 m_2^2 + m_2^2 m_3^2 + m_3^2 m_1^2$ becomes maximum (= 1/3) when $m_1 = m_2 = m_3 = 1/\sqrt{3}$. **(1.5 marks)**
(b) **n-type Si:** Maximum piezoresistance is along the **⟨100⟩ direction** (cube edge), because for n-type Si the longitudinal coefficient $\pi_{11}$ is maximum along the principal axis. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the relationship $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = (1+2\nu)\varepsilon + \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$ for a resistor under uniaxial stress. Explain why the piezoresistive term dominates in doped silicon. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A p-type silicon piezoresistor is oriented along the ⟨110⟩ direction on a (100) wafer. The applied stress is also along ⟨110⟩. Given $\pi_{11} = 6.6 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$, $\pi_{12} = -1.1 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$, and $\pi_{44} = 138.1 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$, calculate the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_l$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

Step 1: $R = \rho L / A$. **(1 mark)**

Step 2: Logarithmic differentiation: $dR/R = d\rho/\rho + dL/L - dA/A$. **(1 mark)**

Step 3: Use $dL/L = \varepsilon$ (axial strain) and $dA/A = -2\nu\varepsilon$ (lateral contraction). **(1.5 marks)**

Step 4: Substitute to obtain $\Delta R/R = (1+2\nu)\varepsilon + \Delta\rho/\rho$. **(1.5 marks)**

Step 5: For Si, $\Delta\rho/\rho$ is large because stress modifies the band structure (carrier repopulation among valleys in n-Si, warping of valence band in p-Si), so the piezoresistive term dominates the geometric term by two orders of magnitude. **(2 marks)**

**(b) — 7 marks**

Step 1: Direction cosines for ⟨110⟩: $m_1 = m_2 = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $m_3 = 0$. So $\vec{m} = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}, 0)$ and similarly $\vec{l} = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}, 0)$. **(1.5 marks)**

Step 2: Compute the orientation factor:
$$F = l_1^2 m_1^2 + l_2^2 m_2^2 + l_3^2 m_3^2 = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 \times 2 + 0 = 2 \times \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$$ **(2 marks)**

Step 3: Calculate the combination of coefficients:
$$\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \frac{\pi_{44}}{2} = 6.6 - (-1.1) - \frac{138.1}{2} = 7.7 - 69.05 = -61.35 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

Step 4: Apply the longitudinal formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
\pi_l &= \pi_{11} - 2\left(\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \frac{\pi_{44}}{2}\right) F \\
&= 6.6 \times 10^{-11} - 2(-61.35 \times 10^{-11})(0.5) \\
&= 6.6 \times 10^{-11} + 61.35 \times 10^{-11} \\
&= 67.95 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$ **(2 marks)**

Final Answer: $\pi_l \approx 68 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$ along ⟨110⟩. This is intermediate between the ⟨100⟩ value and the ⟨111⟩ maximum, as expected.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the working of a piezoresistive pressure sensor. Discuss the role of Wheatstone bridge configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare silicon and gallium arsenide as materials for piezoresistive MEMS sensors. In which application domains does GaAs outperform Si, and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

Step 1: A silicon diaphragm of thickness $h$ is etched anisotropically (e.g., KOH etching of (100) wafer) to form a thin square or circular membrane. **(1.5 marks)**

Step 2: Four p-type piezoresistors are ion-implanted near the edges of the diaphragm where stress is maximum. Two are oriented along the ⟨110⟩ direction (parallel to the wafer edge, experiencing longitudinal stress) and two along the perpendicular direction (transverse stress). **(2 marks)**

Step 3: When differential pressure $P$ is applied, the diaphragm deflects, generating in-plane stresses $\sigma_l$ and $\sigma_t$ at the resistor locations. The resistance changes per the piezoresistive relation. **(1.5 marks)**

Step 4: The four resistors are connected in a Wheatstone bridge. As pressure is applied, two resistors increase and two decrease in resistance, producing a differential output voltage:
$$V_{\text{out}} \approx \frac{V_s}{2}(\pi_l \sigma_l - \pi_t \sigma_t)$$
This configuration provides temperature compensation (since all four resistors change identically with temperature) and doubles the sensitivity compared to a single-resistor readout. **(2 marks)**

**(b) — 7 marks**

Step 1: Both Si and GaAs are cubic semiconductors with piezoresistive behavior, but their properties differ. **(1 mark)**

Step 2: Silicon has higher gauge factor (50–200) than GaAs (−5 to −50 in magnitude), so Si gives larger signal for the same stress. **(1 mark)**

Step 3: GaAs has wider bandgap (1.42 eV vs 1.12 eV) and remains piezoresistively active up to ~400 °C, whereas Si sensors degrade above ~150 °C. GaAs therefore wins in **high-temperature applications** (aerospace engine monitoring, oil/gas downhole, nuclear reactors). **(1.5 marks)**

Step 4: GaAs is a direct-bandgap semiconductor, enabling **monolithic integration of optical sources/detectors** with MEMS — useful in **opto-MEMS** (optical switches, tunable lasers, bio-sensing with fluorescence). **(1.5 marks)**

Step 5: GaAs is intrinsically **piezoelectric** (zinc-blende structure lacks inversion symmetry), whereas Si is not. This allows GaAs to be used as both a piezoresistive and piezoelectric transducer. **(1 mark)**

Step 6: GaAs has higher electron mobility (8500 vs 1400 cm²/V·s), advantageous for **RF MEMS** and high-frequency applications. **(1 mark)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up ⟨100⟩ and ⟨111⟩:** Saying p-type Si has maximum piezoresistance along ⟨100⟩ (wrong — that's n-type). Costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the orientation factor $F$:** Many students write $\pi_l = \pi_{11}$ for "any orientation". This is only true for ⟨100⟩. For other directions, the full formula with $F$ must be used. Costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Unit errors:** The coefficient $\pi$ has units of $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ or $\text{m}^2/\text{N}$. Students often omit units entirely or write $\text{Ω}$ (wrong dimension). Costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Conflating gauge factor with piezoresistive coefficient:** $G$ is dimensionless; $\pi$ has units. They are related by $G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$ but are not the same quantity. Costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing piezoresistive with piezoelectric effect:** Piezoresistive = change in resistance; piezoelectric = generation of charge/voltage. GaAs exhibits both, Si exhibits only the first. Costs 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the log-differentiation step:** In derivation questions, the step $dR/R = d\rho/\rho + dL/L - dA/A$ must be shown explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (Silicon Piezoresistors, GaAs)**
>
> - **Piezoresistive effect definition:** $\Delta R / R = (1+2\nu)\varepsilon + \Delta \rho / \rho$. The piezoresistive term dominates in Si. **(Core)**
> - **Three independent coefficients** for cubic crystals: $\pi_{11}, \pi_{12}, \pi_{44}$. For p-type Si, $\pi_{44}$ is largest. **(Core)**
> - **Maximum sensitivity direction:**
>   * p-type Si → ⟨111⟩
>   * n-type Si → ⟨100⟩
> - **Longitudinal and transverse coefficient formulas** with orientation factor $F = l_1^2 m_1^2 + l_2^2 m_2^2 + l_3^2 m_3^2$. **(Frequently tested)**
> - **Gauge factor** $G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$ ranges from **50 to 200** in silicon (vs 2 in metals). **(Frequently tested)**
> - **Doping effect:** Higher doping → lower piezoresistive coefficient (trade-off with temperature stability).
> - **Temperature effect:** TCP typically **−0.1 to −0.3 %/°C**. Wheatstone bridge compensates common-mode temperature drift.
> - **GaAs properties:**
>   * Bandgap **1.42 eV** (direct)
>   * Electron mobility **8500 cm²/V·s**
>   * High-temperature operation up to **400 °C**
>   * Inherently **piezoelectric** (zinc-blende lacks inversion symmetry)
> - **Application domains:**
>   * Si: pressure sensors, accelerometers (low-cost, high-volume)
>   * GaAs: high-T sensors, RF MEMS, opto-MEMS, harsh environments
> - **Wheatstone bridge output:** $V_{\text{out}} = (V_s/2)(\pi_l \sigma_l - \pi_t \sigma_t)$ for full-bridge configuration.
> - **Fabrication:** Ion implantation of boron (p-type) or phosphorus/arsenic (n-type) on (100) Si, followed by KOH/TMAH anisotropic etching to form diaphragm.
> - **Key historical fact:** C.S. Smith (1954) first reported the strong piezoresistive effect in Ge and Si, leading to modern MEMS pressure sensors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quartz (SiO₂)** is a single-crystalline, naturally occurring or synthetically grown piezoelectric material belonging to the trigonal crystal system (point group 32). It is the most widely used piezoelectric substrate in MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mech

topics.title: Piezoelectric Crystals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT632 - MICRO-ELECTRO-MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Crystals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Piezoelectric Crystals

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectricity (Greek: piezein = to press; piezo = pressure):** It is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials to convert **mechanical energy** (force, stress, strain, pressure, vibration) into **electrical energy** (charge, voltage, electric field), and conversely, to convert **electrical energy** into precise mechanical deformation. Materials exhibiting this property are termed **piezoelectric crystals** or **piezoelectrics**.

In the strict crystallographic sense, piezoelectricity is a **linear electromechanical coupling phenomenon** that arises only in crystals belonging to one of the **20 non-centrosymmetric point groups** out of the 32 crystallographic point groups. The remaining 11 centrosymmetric groups plus the cubic group with inversion center cannot display the direct piezoelectric effect.

**KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** The piezoelectric effect is one of the three primary "smart material" transduction mechanisms (alongside piezoresistivity and electrostatic actuation) used as the foundational operating principle in RF-MEMS resonators, acoustic sensors, inkjet printheads, energy harvesters, and atomic force microscopy (AFM) cantilevers.

## 1.2 The Two Directions of the Effect

There are exactly two reciprocal manifestations of piezoelectricity:

### (a) Direct Piezoelectric Effect (Sensor Mode)

When a mechanical force (stress, σ) is applied to a piezoelectric crystal, equal and opposite electrical charges appear on opposing faces of the crystal.

- **Input:** Mechanical stress σ [N/m²]
- **Output:** Electrical charge density D [C/m²] or surface charge Q [C]

This is the principle used in **force, pressure, and vibration sensors**, microphones, and acoustic pickups.

### (b) Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect (Actuator Mode)

When an electric field **E** [V/m] is applied across a properly oriented piezoelectric crystal, the crystal undergoes a precisely controlled mechanical strain (deformation).

- **Input:** Electric field E [V/m]
- **Output:** Mechanical strain S [dimensionless]

This is the principle used in **precision actuators**, ultrasonic transducers, nanopositioning stages, and piezoelectric inkjet printheads.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a squeeze-bottle filled with two immiscible liquids (oil and water) separated by a thin membrane with positively charged ions on one side and negatively charged ions on the other (a dipole).**
>
> - **Normal state (no squeezing):** The positive and negative charges sit neatly aligned inside, producing **no net voltage** across the electrodes attached to the bottle's outer walls.
> - **When you squeeze the bottle (mechanical input):** The dipole layers shift relative to each other. Positive charges move closer to one electrode and negative charges move closer to the other — a **voltage appears across the electrodes**. That is the **direct effect** (mechanical → electrical).
> - **Now apply a battery voltage across the electrodes (electrical input):** The positive ions are pulled toward the negative electrode and negative ions toward the positive electrode, slightly deforming the membrane and changing the bottle's shape. That is the **converse effect** (electrical → mechanical).
>
> The piezoelectric crystal behaves exactly like this "squeeze-bottle with built-in dipoles" — squeeze it, electricity flows; electrify it, it moves.

## 1.4 Why Only Certain Crystals Are Piezoelectric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental requirement for piezoelectricity is the **absence of a center of symmetry (centrosymmetry)** in the crystal lattice. In a centrosymmetric crystal, the internal charge distribution is symmetric about a central point, so any applied stress produces equal and opposite displacements that **cancel each other out**, yielding zero net polarization. In non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., quartz, tourmaline, lithium niobate), the centers of positive and negative charge **do not coincide**, so a stress shifts them asymmetrically, producing a net dipole moment per unit volume — the piezoelectric polarization.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following quantities form the "alphabet" of piezoelectric material characterization and are universally tested in KTU examinations:

- **Piezoelectric strain constant (d):** C/N or m/V; quantifies strain produced per unit electric field or charge produced per unit stress.
- **Piezoelectric voltage constant (g):** V·m/N; quantifies electric field produced per unit applied stress.
- **Piezoelectric stress constant (e):** N/V·m or C/m²; used in stress–electric field formulations.
- **Piezoelectric stiffness constant (h):** N/C or V/m; used in strain–electric displacement formulations.
- **Electromechanical coupling factor (k):** **dimensionless**, lies between **0 and 1** (ideally 1); represents the efficiency of energy conversion between mechanical and electrical domains.
- **Relative permittivity (εᵣ):** dimensionless; typically 4–2000 depending on the material class.
- **Curie temperature (Tc):** °C or K; above this temperature, the crystal loses its piezoelectric property (transitions to a paraelectric phase).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crystallographic Origin of Piezoelectricity

Of the **32 crystallographic point groups**, **11 possess a center of symmetry** (centrosymmetric) and **21 lack a center of symmetry** (non-centrosymmetric). Of these 21 non-centrosymmetric groups, **one group (the cubic point group 432)** is non-piezoelectric due to other symmetry constraints, leaving exactly **20 piezoelectric point groups**.

The piezoelectric point groups commonly encountered in KTU syllabus are:

| Crystal System | Piezoelectric Point Groups |
|---|---|
| Triclinic | 1 |
| Monoclinic | 2, m |
| Orthorhombic | 222, mm2 |
| Tetragonal | 4, 4̄, 422, 4mm |
| Trigonal (Rhombohedral) | 3, 32, 3m |
| Hexagonal | 6, 6̄, 622, 6mm |
| Cubic | 23, 4̄3m (only 2 of the 5 non-centrosymmetric cubic groups) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quartz** belongs to point group **32** (trigonal). **Lithium Niobate (LiNbO₃)** belongs to **3m**. **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** ceramics, when poled, exhibit effective symmetry **∞m** (transversely isotropic), and **Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF)** is an oriented semi-crystalline polymer.

## 2.2 Mathematical Framework — Tensor Notation

Piezoelectricity is fundamentally a **second-rank tensor coupling** between a second-rank mechanical tensor (stress σ or strain S) and a first-rank polar vector (electric field **E** or electric displacement **D**).

The **linear constitutive equations** that govern all piezoelectric phenomena are:

### Equation Set 1 — Stress–Charge Form (Sensor Equations)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i &= s^{E}_{ij}\,\sigma_j + d_{in}\,E_n \\
D_m &= d_{mj}\,\sigma_j + \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}\,E_n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Equation Set 2 — Strain–Voltage Form (Actuator Equations)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_i &= c^{E}_{ij}\,S_j - e_{in}\,E_n \\
D_m &= e_{mj}\,S_j + \varepsilon^{S}_{mn}\,E_n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Index Convention (KTU Standard)

- Indices **i, j** = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 — components of strain/stress in **Voigt notation** (1=xx, 2=yy, 3=zz, 4=yz, 5=xz, 6=xy).
- Indices **m, n** = 1, 2, 3 — Cartesian components of electric field/electric displacement.
- **s^E** = elastic compliance at constant electric field [m²/N].
- **c^E** = elastic stiffness at constant electric field [N/m²].
- **d, e** = piezoelectric coefficients [m/V or C/N for d; C/m² or N/V·m for e].
- **ε^σ** = permittivity at constant stress [F/m].
- **ε^S** = permittivity at constant strain [F/m].

## 2.3 Structure of the Piezoelectric Coefficient Matrix

Because of crystal symmetry, the matrix **dᵢₙ** (3×6) has very few independent elements. For example, for trigonal crystals (quartz, point group 32), the **d-matrix** reduces to just **two independent components: d₁₁ and d₁₄**:

$$
[d]_{32} = \begin{bmatrix} d_{11} & -d_{11} & 0 & d_{14} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -d_{14} & -2d_{11} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For poled ceramics (PZT, point group ∞m), the matrix contains **three independent components: d₃₃, d₃₁, d₁₅**:

$$
[d]_{\infty m} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 & 0 \\ d_{31} & d_{31} & d_{33} & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_i = s^{E}_{ij}\sigma_j + d_{in}E_n$ | Converse effect (actuator): strain from stress & E-field | S [–], s [m²/N], σ [N/m²], d [m/V], E [V/m] |
| 2 | $D_m = d_{mj}\sigma_j + \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}E_n$ | Direct effect (sensor): D-field from stress & E-field | D [C/m²], d [C/N], ε [F/m] |
| 3 | $d = \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial E}\right)_{\sigma} = \left(\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial \sigma}\right)_{E}$ | Definition of d-coefficient | C/N or m/V |
| 4 | $g = \dfrac{d}{\varepsilon^{\sigma}} = \dfrac{E}{\sigma}$ | Voltage constant — open-circuit field per stress | V·m/N |
| 5 | $e = \dfrac{d}{s^{E}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ (at S=0) | Stress constant — blocked stress per E-field | C/m² or N/V·m |
| 6 | $k^2 = \dfrac{d^2}{s^{E}\varepsilon^{\sigma}}$ | Electromechanical coupling coefficient (sensor form) | dimensionless, 0 ≤ k ≤ 1 |
| 7 | $k^2_{ij} = \dfrac{d_{ij}^2}{s^{E}_{jj}\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{ii}}$ | Modal coupling factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $k_{31}^2 = \dfrac{d_{31}^2}{s^{E}_{11}\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}}$ | 31-mode coupling (PZT rod poled in 3, electrodes on 1) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $k_{33}^2 = \dfrac{d_{33}^2}{s^{E}_{33}\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}}$ | 33-mode coupling (longitudinal) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}\,s^{E}_{11}}{d_{31}^2} \cdot t$ | Critical thickness for unimorph bending | depends on geometry |
| 11 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\rho\,s^{E}_{11}}}$ | Longitudinal resonance frequency of a bar | Hz |
| 12 | $Q_m = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f}$ | Mechanical quality factor | dimensionless |
| 13 | $S_{piezo} = d_{31}\,\dfrac{V}{t}$ | Induced strain in 31-mode (actuator) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $V_{oc} = g_{33}\,\sigma_3\,t$ | Open-circuit voltage from axial stress | V |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Domain | Effect Used |
|---|---|---|
| Quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) | Mass sensing / RF-MEMS | Direct (resonance shift) |
| Piezoelectric inkjet printhead | Printing | Converse |
| Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) cantilever | Nanopositioning | Converse |
| Ultrasound transducers | Medical imaging | Both |
| Vibration energy harvesters | IoT / wireless sensors | Direct |
| SAW (Surface Acoustic Wave) filters | RF communication | Both |
| Fuel injector actuators | Automotive | Converse |
| Quartz watches | Timekeeping | Both (resonator) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The electromechanical coupling factor **k** is the single most important metric for transducer design. For **k close to 1**, almost all electrical energy converts to mechanical (or vice versa). The highest **k** materials are **relaxor ferroelectric single crystals** (e.g., PMN-PT, k ≈ 0.92), exceeding even PZT (k ≈ 0.5–0.6). For this reason, PMN-PT is increasingly the material of choice in advanced MEMS ultrasonic transducers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Constitutive Equations from Thermodynamic Potential

We begin from the **Gibbs free energy density** G(T, σ, E), where T is temperature, σ is stress, and E is electric field. Expanding G to second order about equilibrium:

$$
G = G_0 - S_j\,\sigma_j - D_m\,E_m - \tfrac{1}{2}\,s^{E}_{ij}\,\sigma_i\sigma_j - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}\,E_mE_n - d_{in}\,\sigma_i\,E_n
$$

By definition of the partial derivatives:

$$
S_i = -\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial \sigma_i}\bigg\vert_E , \qquad D_m = -\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial E_m}\bigg\vert_{\sigma}
$$

Carrying out the differentiation term by term:

$$
S_i = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \sigma_i}\Big(G_0 - S_j\sigma_j - D_mE_m - \tfrac{1}{2}s^{E}_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j - \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}E_mE_n - d_{in}\sigma_iE_n\Big)\bigg\vert_E
$$

Since G₀, DₘEₘ, and ε terms do not depend on σᵢ, they vanish:

$$
S_i = S_i + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \sigma_i}\Big(\tfrac{1}{2}s^{E}_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j\Big) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \sigma_i}(d_{in}\sigma_iE_n)
$$

Evaluating the partial derivatives:

$$
S_i = S_i + s^{E}_{ij}\,\sigma_j + d_{in}\,E_n
$$

Cancelling Sᵢ from both sides (it appears identically) we obtain the **converse piezoelectric equation**:

$$
S_i = s^{E}_{ij}\,\sigma_j + d_{in}\,E_n
$$

Similarly for Dₘ:

$$
D_m = d_{mj}\,\sigma_j + \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}\,E_n
$$

This is the **direct piezoelectric equation** — completing the derivation of the **stress–charge form** of the constitutive relations from first principles.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Coupling Coefficient from Energy Balance

Consider a piezoelectric element subjected to a cyclic stress σ at zero applied electric field (open circuit). The energy density stored is:

$$
U_{mech} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,s^{E}\,\sigma^2
$$

The energy density stored in the electrical domain (the polarization induced by σ) is:

$$
U_{elec} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{d^2\,\sigma^2}{\varepsilon^{\sigma}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{(d\sigma)^2}{\varepsilon^{\sigma}}
$$

The **electromechanical coupling factor k** is defined as the square root of the ratio of stored electrical energy to input mechanical energy:

$$
k^2 = \dfrac{U_{elec}}{U_{mech}} = \dfrac{\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{d^2\sigma^2}{\varepsilon^{\sigma}}}{\frac{1}{2}s^{E}\sigma^2}
$$

Simplifying the numerator and denominator:

$$
k^2 = \dfrac{d^2\sigma^2}{s^{E}\sigma^2\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}} = \dfrac{d^2}{s^{E}\varepsilon^{\sigma}}
$$

Therefore, the **electromechanical coupling coefficient** in the sensor form is:

$$
k^2 = \dfrac{d^2}{s^{E}\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}}
$$

For the **31-mode** of a poled ceramic (PZT), this specializes to:

$$
k^2_{31} = \dfrac{d^2_{31}}{s^E_{11}\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Charge from Applied Force (Sensor Use-Case)

For a piezoelectric slab poled along axis 3 with electrodes perpendicular to axis 3, the **33-mode** is engaged. The induced electric displacement D₃ from an applied stress σ₃ is:

$$
D_3 = d_{33}\,\sigma_3 + \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}\,E_3
$$

Under **open-circuit conditions**, the integral of D over the electrode area equals the surface charge Q, and the average E-field satisfies V = E₃·t (where t is slab thickness). The total free charge on the electrodes is:

$$
Q = D_3 \cdot A = d_{33}\,\sigma_3 \cdot A + \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}\,E_3\cdot A
$$

Under open-circuit (no current can flow), the displacement current integral is zero, which for a static force gives E₃ → 0 (no charge buildup across the capacitor if no external path):

$$
\boxed{\,Q = d_{33}\,F\,}
$$

where F = σ₃·A is the applied force. This is the **fundamental sensor equation** of a piezoelectric force sensor: **charge is directly proportional to applied force**, with proportionality constant d₃₃ [C/N].

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Strain from Applied Voltage (Actuator Use-Case)

For the converse effect in **31-mode**, electrodes are on faces perpendicular to axis 1, but the poling is along axis 3. A voltage V applied across the electrodes of thickness t produces E₃ = V/t. Under zero mechanical stress (free boundary):

$$
S_1 = s^E_{11}\cdot 0 + d_{31}\,E_3 = d_{31}\,\dfrac{V}{t}
$$

Therefore the **induced strain in the 1-direction is**:

$$
S_1 = \dfrac{d_{31}\,V}{t}
$$

The corresponding **free displacement δ** of a bar of length L along axis 1:

$$
\delta = S_1\cdot L = \dfrac{d_{31}\,V\,L}{t}
$$

For PZT-5A, d₃₁ ≈ −1.71 × 10⁻¹⁰ m/V. With V = 100 V, L = 10 mm, t = 1 mm:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{(1.71\times 10^{-10})(100)(0.01)}{0.001} = 1.71\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 171\,\text{nm}
$$

This nanometer-scale precision is what makes piezoelectric actuators so valuable in scanning probe microscopy and nanopositioning stages.

## 3.5 Derivation V — Frequency Shift of a Quartz Resonator

For an AT-cut quartz thickness-shear resonator of thickness t, the resonant frequency in vacuum is:

$$
f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\dfrac{c_{66}}{\rho}}
$$

where **c₆₆** is the shear stiffness, **ρ** is density. When a thin foreign film of mass m is added, the frequency decreases by Sauerbrey's relation:

$$
\Delta f = -\dfrac{2f_0^2}{\sqrt{\rho\,c_{66}}}\cdot\dfrac{m}{A}
$$

This is the foundational principle of the **Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM)** — a MEMS device used to measure sub-nanogram mass changes in thin films.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> All node identifiers below are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `flowB`), all labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, and no reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node IDs.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Transduction Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[MECHANICAL DOMAIN]:::mech
    nodeB[STRESS SIGMA]:::mech
    nodeC[CRYSTAL LATTICE]:::center
    nodeD[ELECTRIC FIELD E]:::elec
    nodeE[ELECTRICAL DOMAIN]:::elec
    nodeF[CHARGE DENSITY D]:::elec
    nodeG[STRAIN S]:::mech
    nodeH[POLARIZATION P]:::center

    nodeJ[CONVERSE EFFECT PATH]:::center
    nodeK[OUTPUT ELECTRICAL SIGNAL]:::elec
    nodeL[OUTPUT MECHANICAL MOTION]:::mech

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeE

    nodeE --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeA

    nodeC -.-> nodeH
    nodeH -.-> nodeI
    nodeH -.-> nodeJ
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the direct and converse piezoelectric effects with a neat sketch.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark) Direct Piezoelectric Effect:** When a mechanical stress σ is applied to a non-centrosymmetric crystal (e.g., quartz, PZT), the centers of positive and negative charge within the unit cell separate, generating a net electric displacement D and surface charge Q. The governing equation is $D_m = d_{mj}\,\sigma_j + \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}\,E_n$.

- **(1 mark) Converse Piezoelectric Effect:** When an external electric field E is applied, the crystal undergoes a mechanical strain S proportional to E, given by $S_i = s^E_{ij}\,\sigma_j + d_{in}\,E_n$. At zero mechanical stress, S = d·E.

- **(1 mark) Sketch & Example:** A labeled diagram should show the crystal between two electrodes. Examples: direct effect → quartz force sensor; converse effect → PZT ultrasonic transducer.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Differentiate between centrosymmetric and non-centrosymmetric crystals. Why are only the latter piezoelectric?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark) Definition:** A **centrosymmetric** crystal possesses a center of inversion symmetry — every atom at position (x, y, z) has an identical atom at (-x, -y, -z). A **non-centrosymmetric** crystal lacks this inversion center.

- **(1 mark) Piezoelectric Reasoning:** In centrosymmetric crystals, an applied stress produces charge displacements that are symmetric about the center, so equal positive and negative charges accumulate at the same point → **net polarization = 0** → no piezoelectric effect.

- **(1 mark) Non-centrosymmetric Case:** In crystals such as quartz (point group 32) or PZT (after poling), the lack of inversion allows the stress to shift positive and negative sublattices asymmetrically → **net dipole moment per unit volume** → measurable surface charge. Of 32 point groups, only 20 are piezoelectric.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students commonly lose marks by **forgetting to specify units** for d, e, g, h coefficients. Always write units explicitly (e.g., "d₃₃ = 400 pC/N" not just "400").
> 2. **Do not confuse "poling direction" with "applied electric field direction"** — after poling, the spontaneous polarization is permanent and is denoted Pₛ.
> 3. When writing the d-matrix, **always preserve the row-column convention** — rows correspond to electric field component (i = 1, 2, 3), columns correspond to strain/stress component in Voigt notation (j = 1…6).
> 4. For derivation-type questions, marks are awarded for **each intermediate step**, not just the final result. Skipping the tensor reduction step is a frequent reason for 50% mark loss.
> 5. **Never confuse "electrostrictive" (quadratic in E) with "piezoelectric" (linear in E)** — electromechanical strain S = M·E² applies to all centrosymmetric materials but is much weaker.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024, Module 3]**

> *(a)* Derive the **stress–charge form of the linear constitutive equations** of piezoelectricity starting from the Gibbs free energy density. Explain each term in your final expression. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* For a PZT ceramic (poled along axis 3), write the complete **d-matrix**, identify the three independent piezoelectric constants, and explain the physical meaning of the **31-mode and 33-mode coupling factors k₃₁ and k₃₃**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A(a) — Derivation of Constitutive Equations (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the second-order expansion of Gibbs free energy density:

$$
G(T,\sigma,E) = G_0 - S_j\sigma_j - D_mE_m - \tfrac{1}{2}s^E_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j - \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}E_mE_n - d_{in}\sigma_iE_n
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the thermodynamic definitions:

$$
S_i = -\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial \sigma_i}\bigg\vert_E \qquad D_m = -\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial E_m}\bigg\vert_{\sigma}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the partial derivatives. Differentiate G with respect to σᵢ while holding E constant:

$$
\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial \sigma_i} = -S_j\delta_{ij} - s^E_{ij}\sigma_j - d_{in}E_n
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the negative sign to get the strain equation:

$$
S_i = S_i + s^E_{ij}\sigma_j + d_{in}E_n \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; S_i = s^E_{ij}\sigma_j + d_{in}E_n
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Repeat for Dₘ:

$$
\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial E_m} = -D_m - \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}E_n - d_{in}\sigma_i\delta_{mn}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\; D_m = d_{mi}\sigma_i + \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}E_n
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** State the final boxed equations and explain each term:

$$
\boxed{\;S_i = s^E_{ij}\sigma_j + d_{in}E_n\;}
$$
$$
\boxed{\;D_m = d_{mj}\sigma_j + \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}E_n\;}
$$

- **s^E** (compliance at constant E): mechanical compliance contribution to strain from stress.
- **d** (piezoelectric coefficient): cross-coupling — converts mechanical to electrical domain.
- **ε^σ** (permittivity at constant σ): pure electrical response.

### Model Solution A(b) — PZT d-matrix and Coupling Factors (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Write the d-matrix for point group ∞m (poled ceramic):

$$
[d]_{\infty m} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 & 0 \\ d_{31} & d_{31} & d_{33} & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Identify three independent constants:

- **d₃₁** — transverse coefficient: stress along axis 1 induces polarization along axis 3, or electric field along axis 3 induces strain along axis 1.
- **d₃₃** — longitudinal coefficient: stress along axis 3 induces polarization along axis 3, or electric field along axis 3 induces strain along axis 3.
- **d₁₅** — shear coefficient: shear stress in xz-plane produces polarization along axis 1, or electric field along axis 1 produces shear strain.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Define the modal coupling factors:

$$
k^2_{31} = \dfrac{d^2_{31}}{s^E_{11}\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}} \qquad k^2_{33} = \dfrac{d^2_{33}}{s^E_{33}\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Physical meaning:
- **k₃₁:** Efficiency of energy conversion in a thin plate poled through thickness (axis 3) with electrodes on major faces (axis 1). Field ⊥ poling.
- **k₃₃:** Efficiency in a rod poled and stressed/electrified along its length. Field ∥ poling. Always k₃₃ > k₃₁.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

> *(a)* A piezoelectric force sensor uses a PZT-5A slab of area A = 100 mm² and thickness t = 2 mm. A force F = 50 N is applied along the poling direction (33-mode). Given: **d₃₃ = 400 × 10⁻¹² C/N**, **εᵣ = 1800**, **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**. Calculate: (i) the open-circuit voltage generated, (ii) the charge produced, and (iii) the electromechanical coupling coefficient if s^E₃₃ = 18 × 10⁻¹² m²/N. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **piezoelectric vibration energy harvester** for wireless sensor nodes, identifying each functional block and the role of the electromechanical coupling coefficient in determining output power. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B(a) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute stress:

$$
\sigma_3 = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{50}{100\times 10^{-6}} = 5\times 10^{5}\,\text{N/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the open-circuit voltage using:

$$
V_{oc} = g_{33}\,\sigma_3\,t = \dfrac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}}\,\sigma_3\,t
$$

Compute permittivity:

$$
\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33} = \varepsilon_r\,\varepsilon_0 = 1800 \times 8.854\times 10^{-12} = 1.594\times 10^{-8}\,\text{F/m}
$$

Compute voltage:

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{400\times 10^{-12}}{1.594\times 10^{-8}}\times 5\times 10^5\times 2\times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{400\times 10^{-12}\times 5\times 10^5 \times 2\times 10^{-3}}{1.594\times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{4\times 10^{-7}}{1.594\times 10^{-8}} \approx 25.1\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute charge:

$$
Q = d_{33}\,F = 400\times 10^{-12}\times 50 = 2\times 10^{-8}\,\text{C} = 20\,\text{nC}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Coupling coefficient:

$$
k^2_{33} = \dfrac{d^2_{33}}{s^E_{33}\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}} = \dfrac{(400\times 10^{-12})^2}{(18\times 10^{-12})(1.594\times 10^{-8})}
$$

$$
k^2_{33} = \dfrac{1.6\times 10^{-19}}{2.87\times 10^{-19}} \approx 0.557 \;\Rightarrow\; k_{33} \approx 0.746
$$

**Interpretation:** A k₃₃ of 0.75 means about **56%** of input mechanical energy is converted to electrical energy in a single cycle — a typical value for PZT-5A.

### Model Solution B(b) — Piezoelectric Vibration Energy Harvester (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Block diagram and functional blocks (already shown in Section 4.3). Describe each block:

1. **Mass-spring mechanical resonator:** tuned to ambient vibration frequency ωₐ by choosing mass m and spring constant k_piezo.
2. **Piezoelectric layer (PZT):** converts cyclic stress into AC voltage via direct effect.
3. **Interdigital / top-bottom electrodes:** harvest the induced charge.
4. **Rectifier bridge (Schottky diodes):** converts AC → DC; Schottky preferred for low forward drop (~0.3 V).
5. **Filter capacitor:** smooths rectified DC ripple.
6. **Voltage regulator (LDO or buck converter):** delivers stable 3.3 V to load.
7. **Storage element (supercapacitor or Li-ion micro-battery):** buffers harvested energy.
8. **Load:** wireless sensor node (e.g., BLE, LoRa, ZigBee).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Role of coupling coefficient in power output:

The harvested power is:

$$
P_{harv} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,k^2_{eff}\,m\,Y_p\,\varepsilon^{\sigma}_{33}\,\omega_a\,S_{max}
$$

or in canonical form:

$$
P \propto k^2_{i}\cdot F^2\cdot \omega_a
$$

Higher **k** → more efficient energy conversion → more harvested power. **PMN-PT (k ≈ 0.92) can deliver 5–10× the power of PZT (k ≈ 0.6) at the same excitation.**

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Frequency-matching requirement and bandwidth:

The harvester must resonate at ambient frequency:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{mech}}{m_{eff}}}
$$

Maximum power transfer occurs at resonance when input frequency = ωₙ. Bandwidth Δω is governed by Q-factor: Δω = ωₙ/Q. Q = 1/(2ζ) where ζ is damping ratio. Trade-off: high Q → narrow bandwidth but high peak power.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Typical output: A PZT cantilever (50×10×0.5 mm³) under 1 g acceleration at 50 Hz can harvest **10–100 μW**, sufficient to periodically power a wireless sensor transmitting every few seconds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Piezoelectric Crystals**

- ✅ Piezoelectricity exists only in **20 non-centrosymmetric point groups** out of 32.
- ✅ **Direct effect:** mechanical → electrical (sensor). **Converse effect:** electrical → mechanical (actuator).
- ✅ **Linear constitutive equations (stress–charge form):**
   - $S_i = s^E_{ij}\sigma_j + d_{in}E_n$
   - $D_m = d_{mj}\sigma_j + \varepsilon^{\sigma}_{mn}E_n$
- ✅ Four primary piezoelectric constants: **d, e, g, h** — all interrelated.
- ✅ **Coupling factor:** $k^2 = d^2/(s^E \varepsilon^\sigma)$ — dimensionless, always **0 ≤ k ≤ 1**.
- ✅ **31-mode** (lateral actuation) vs **33-mode** (longitudinal actuation) — always k₃₃ > k₃₁.
- ✅ **Quartz** is point group **32** with d-matrix containing only d₁₁ and d₁₄.
- ✅ **PZT** (poled ceramic) has effective symmetry **∞m** with d₃₁, d₃₃, d₁₅ independent.
- ✅ **Curie temperature** Tc = upper limit of piezoelectric operation; above Tc, material is **paraelectric**.
- ✅ **Sauerbrey equation** governs QCM mass sensing: Δf ∝ −Δm.
- ✅ **PMN-PT** has the highest known k (≈ 0.92), used in advanced ultrasound and energy harvesting.
- ✅ For an actuator, free displacement: δ = d·V·L/t. For a sensor, charge: Q = d·F.
- ✅ **Units to memorize cold:** d [C/N or m/V], g [V·m/N], e [C/m² or N/V·m], h [N/C or V/m].
- ✅ In MEMS, piezoelectric thin films (PZT, AlN, ScAlN) are deposited by **sputtering, sol-gel, or MOCVD**.
- ✅ **AlN** is CMOS-compatible (no Pb, no ferroelectric hysteresis) — preferred for RF-MEMS and FBAR filters.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT632 - MICRO-ELECTRO-MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Polymers** in the context of Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) are long-chain macromolecular materials composed of repeating structural units called *monomers*, which are strategically employed as functional structural, sacrificial, or sensing elements in microfabricated devices. In KTU's 2024 scheme, polymers are recognized as the **second most important class of MEMS materials** after silicon, owing to their unique combination of mechanical flexibility, chemical tunability, biocompatibility, and low-cost processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A polymer is a high-molecular-weight compound (typically $M_w > 10^4$ g/mol) formed by the covalent linkage of repeating monomer units, exhibiting viscoelastic behavior intermediate between elastic solids and viscous fluids.

The key polymer materials that find explicit mention in the KTU 2024 MEMS syllabus are:

- **SU-8** – Negative-tone epoxy-based photoresist
- **PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane)** – Silicone elastomer
- **PMMA (Poly(methyl methacrylate))** – Thermoplastic positive photoresist
- **Polyimide** – High-temperature aromatic polymer
- **Parylene-C** – Conformal CVD-deposited polymer
- **PEDOT:PSS** – Conductive polymer for flexible electrodes

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a **freight train**. Each wagon is a **monomer** (small unit), and the entire train is the **polymer** (long chain). Just as you can build trains of different lengths, polymers of different chain lengths exhibit dramatically different properties — short chains behave like **hard candy** (brittle plastics), medium chains like **soft rubber** (elastomers), and very long chains like **honey** (viscous fluids).

For MEMS engineers, polymers are like the **"playdough of microsystems"** — they can be molded, spun, cured, stamped, or chemically deposited into microstructures that would be impossible (or prohibitively expensive) to fabricate in silicon.

## 1.3 Why Polymers Matter in MEMS

| Property | Engineering Significance | KTU-Relevant Value |
|----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Young's Modulus (E)** | $E \approx 0.5 - 5$ GPa | 100× lower than Si ($\sim 170$ GPa) → flexible structures |
| **Density (ρ)** | $\rho \approx 1000$ kg/m³ | Lightweight microstructures |
| **Glass Transition Temp (T_g)** | $40$°C – $400$°C | Defines usable thermal range |
| **Biocompatibility** | FDA-approved grades | Implantable biomedical MEMS |
| **Cost** | $<\$5$/wafer | Disposable Lab-on-Chip devices |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "**P**olymers **A**re **L**ight, **F**lexible, **C**heap, **B**io" → **PALS-FCB**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Polymers for MEMS Applications

### A. Based on Origin / Chemistry
- **Natural Polymers**: Cellulose, collagen (limited MEMS use)
- **Synthetic Polymers**: SU-8, PDMS, PMMA, polyimide (dominant in MEMS)

### B. Based on Thermal Response (CRITICAL for KTU)
1. **Thermoplastics** – Soften on heating, harden on cooling (reversible). Example: **PMMA**.
2. **Thermosets** – Irreversibly harden via crosslinking. Examples: **SU-8**, **PDMS** (after curing).

### C. Based on Mechanical Behavior
- **Elastomers** – Large elastic strain ($> 200\%$). Example: **PDMS**.
- **Glass polymers** – Rigid, brittle below $T_g$. Example: **SU-8**.

## 2.2 The "Why and How" of Polymer Behavior in MEMS

**Why do polymers behave differently from silicon?**
- Silicon has a *covalent crystalline network* — bonds are directional and stiff.
- Polymers have *long chains* held by weak van der Waals forces + covalent backbone — chains can uncoil, slide, and rotate.

**How does this affect MEMS?**
- Polymers exhibit **viscoelasticity** — they store energy (elastic) AND dissipate it (viscous) simultaneously.
- This means MEMS polymer devices show **time-dependent deformation** (creep) and **frequency-dependent stiffness** (damping).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|--------|
| 1 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's law (linear regime) | Pa |
| 2 | $E \approx 3 k_B T \cdot \rho / M_w$ | Rubber elasticity (PDMS-like) | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ | Maxwell viscoelastic model | Pa |
| 4 | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Kelvin-Voigt creep strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\tau = \eta / E$ | Characteristic relaxation time | s |
| 6 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion (bimetallic MEMS) | m |
| 7 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^*}}$ | Resonant frequency of polymer cantilever | Hz |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{E \cdot w \cdot t^3}{4 L^3}$ | Stiffness of polymer micro-cantilever | N/m |
| 9 | $\tan \delta = E'' / E'$ | Loss tangent (damping metric) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{sp} = \dfrac{\eta - \eta_0}{\eta_0}$ | Specific viscosity (for spin coating) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Formula #3 and #4 (Maxwell & Kelvin-Voigt) are **favourite KTU questions** in Module 3. Memorize both differential forms AND the step-response solutions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Polymer | MEMS Application | Industrial Use |
|---------|------------------|----------------|
| **SU-8** | High-aspect-ratio microstructures, microchannels | Bio-MEMS, optofluidics |
| **PDMS** | Soft microfluidics, flexible electrodes, skin sensors | Wearable health monitors |
| **PMMA** | X-ray lithography, lab-on-chip substrates | Medical diagnostics (COVID-19 RT-PCR chips) |
| **Polyimide** | Flexible circuits, high-T stress sensors | Aerospace MEMS |
| **Parylene-C** | Conformal encapsulation, neural probes | Implantable neural interfaces |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maxwell Viscoelastic Model (Standard Linear Solid)

The Maxwell model represents a polymer as a **spring (E)** and **dashpot (η)** in series. Under a constant stress $\sigma_0$, it predicts **stress relaxation** — critical for polymer MEMS reliability.

**Step 1: Set up the strain compatibility (series element)**

Since strain adds for elements in series:

$$
\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}
$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to time**

$$
\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

**Step 3: Apply constant strain (clamped boundary condition)**

For a clamped MEMS structure, $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

**Step 4: Rearrange into a first-order ODE**

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta}\sigma = 0
$$

**Step 5: Solve the separable ODE**

Separating variables and integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$
\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma'}{\sigma'} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\int_0^t dt'
$$

**Step 6: Obtain the stress relaxation function**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{E \cdot t}{\eta}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau} \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** After time $\tau = \eta/E$, the stress in a polymer MEMS structure relaxes to $\sim 36.8\%$ of its initial value. For PDMS, $\tau \approx 1$ s; for SU-8, $\tau \approx 10^6$ s — explaining why SU-8 holds shape but PDMS creeps visibly.

## 3.2 Derivation: Resonant Frequency of a Polymer Micro-Cantilever

This derivation is a **high-yield KTU question** in MEMS Module 3.

**Step 1: Cantilever stiffness for a rectangular polymer beam**

For a beam of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$:

$$
k = \frac{E \cdot w \cdot t^3}{4 L^3}
$$

**Step 2: Effective mass of the cantilever (distributed load)**

$$
m^* = 0.24 \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot w \cdot t
$$

**Step 3: Apply simple harmonic motion equation**

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m^*}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute k and m\***

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E \cdot w \cdot t^3 / 4 L^3}{0.24 \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot w \cdot t}}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify by canceling w**

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E \cdot t^2}{0.96 \cdot \rho \cdot L^4}}
$$

**Step 6: Final boxed result**

$$
\boxed{f_n = \frac{t}{4\pi L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{0.24\,\rho}}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Polymer vs. Other MEMS Materials

| Property | Silicon (Si) | PDMS | SU-8 | PMMA | Polyimide |
|----------|--------------|------|------|------|-----------|
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 170 | 0.001 – 0.005 | 4.0 | 3.0 | 3.5 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 2330 | 970 | 1190 | 1180 | 1420 |
| $T_g$ (°C) | N/A (crystal) | −125 | > 200 | 105 | 360 |
| Biocompatible | Limited | Excellent | Limited | Good | Good |
| Fabrication | Cleanroom | Cast/Mold | Photolithography | Hot embossing | Spin coat |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Max aspect ratio | 50:1 | 100:1 | 100:1 | 10:1 | 5:1 |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A PDMS micro-cantilever has $L = 200$ µm, $w = 50$ µm, $t = 10$ µm, $E = 2$ MPa, $\rho = 970$ kg/m³. Calculate the resonant frequency.

**Solution:**

$$
f_n = \frac{t}{4\pi L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{0.24\,\rho}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
f_n = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{4\pi (200 \times 10^{-6})^2}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^6}{0.24 \times 970}}
$$

$$
f_n = \frac{10^{-5}}{4\pi \cdot 4 \times 10^{-8}}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^6}{232.8}}
$$

$$
f_n = \frac{10^{-5}}{5.027 \times 10^{-7}}\sqrt{8591.4}
$$

$$
f_n = 19.89 \times 92.69
$$

$$
\boxed{f_n \approx 1843 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.84 \text{ kHz}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow of MEMS Polymers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Polymers for MEMS] --> B[Photoresists]
    A --> C[Elastomers]
    A --> D[High-Performance Polymers]
    A --> E[Conductive Polymers]

    B --> B2[PMMA: Positive Tone]
    C --> C1[PDMS Silicone Elastomer]
    C --> C2[Polyurethane]

    D --> D2[Parylene-C Conformal]
    E --> E1[PEDOT:PSS]
    E --> E2[Polypyrrole]
    B1 --> F1[Biosensors Microchannels]
    C1 --> F2[Soft Microfluidics Wearables]
    D1 --> F3[Aerospace Flexible Electronics]
    D2 --> F4[Neural Implants Coating]
    E1 --> F5[Flexible Electrodes]
```

## 4.2 Polymer MEMS Fabrication Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[Substrate Preparation] --> step2[Spin Coat Polymer]
    step2 --> step3[Soft Bake]
    step3 --> step4[Exposure UV or E-beam]
    step4 --> step5[Post Exposure Bake]
    step5 --> step6[Development]
    step6 --> step7[Hard Bake or Cure]
    step7 --> step8[Release or Assembly]
    step8 --> step9[Polymer MEMS Device]
    step9 --> stepA[Bio MEMS Sensors]
    step9 --> stepB[Lab on Chip]
```
