```mermaid
    step9 --> stepD[Flexible Electronics]
```

## 4.3 Viscoelastic Response Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Maxwell_Model
        m1[Spring E] --- m2[Block Move] --- m3[Apply Stress]
        m3 --> m4[Stress Decays Exponentially]
    end
    subgraph Kelvin_Voigt_Model
        k1[Spring E] --- k2[Dashpot eta Parallel] --- k3[Apply Stress]
        k3 --> k4[Strain Approaches Steady State]
    end
    m4 --> outcome[Viscoelastic Behavior Validated]
    k4 --> outcome
    outcome --> pdms[PDMS: Maxwell Dominant]
    outcome --> su8[SU-8: Kelvin-Voigt Dominant]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "polymer" as used in MEMS. List any four polymers commonly used in microfabrication with one key property of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A polymer is a high-molecular-weight material made of repeating monomer units bonded covalently, exhibiting viscoelastic behavior. Four MEMS polymers:
1. **SU-8** – High aspect ratio (> 20:1) in photolithography
2. **PDMS** – Biocompatible elastomer with $E \approx 2$ MPa
3. **PMMA** – Low-cost thermoplastic, X-ray sensitive
4. **Parylene-C** – Conformal CVD coating, USP Class VI biocompatible
*(1 mark definition + 2 marks listing + 1 mark property = full 3 marks)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between thermoplastics and thermosets. Give one example of each used in MEMS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Thermoplastic | Thermoset |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| Behavior on heating | Softens (reversible) | Hardens irreversibly |
| Crosslinking | None / Linear chains | Permanent 3D network |
| MEMS Example | **PMMA** | **SU-8** |
| Remoldable? | Yes | No |

*(2 marks distinction + 1 mark example = full 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A: (14 Marks)** [Polymer Mechanics & Modeling]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Maxwell** and **Kelvin-Voigt** viscoelastic models for polymers. Derive the stress relaxation equation for a Maxwell element under constant strain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer micro-cantilever of length $L = 300$ µm, width $w = 80$ µm, thickness $t = 15$ µm is fabricated from SU-8 ($E = 4$ GPa, $\rho = 1190$ kg/m³). Calculate:
  (i) The spring constant $k$ of the cantilever
  (ii) The fundamental resonant frequency $f_n$ **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Maxwell & Kelvin-Voigt Models:**

- **Maxwell Model:** Spring (E) and dashpot (η) in **series**. Models **stress relaxation** behavior.
- **Kelvin-Voigt Model:** Spring (E) and dashpot (η) in **parallel**. Models **creep** behavior.

**Derivation** (Maxwell, constant strain):
The compatibility equation for series elements:

$$
\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_E + \varepsilon_\eta
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

For constant strain, $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$, leading to:

$$
\dot{\sigma} + \frac{E}{\eta}\sigma = 0
$$

Solving with initial condition $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0$:

$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}
$$

**(Marks split: 2 for diagrams + 3 for derivation + 2 for explanation = 7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Spring constant:**

$$
k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}
$$

Substituting:

$$
k = \frac{(4 \times 10^9)(80 \times 10^{-6})(15 \times 10^{-6})^3}{4 (300 \times 10^{-6})^3}
$$

$$
k = \frac{(4 \times 10^9)(80 \times 10^{-6})(3.375 \times 10^{-15})}{4 (2.7 \times 10^{-14})}
$$

$$
k = \frac{1.08 \times 10^{-9}}{1.08 \times 10^{-13}} = 1.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ N/m}
$$

**(ii) Resonant frequency:**

Effective mass:

$$
m^* = 0.24 \rho L w t = 0.24 \times 1190 \times 300 \times 10^{-6} \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 15 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
m^* = 0.24 \times 1190 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-13} = 1.028 \times 10^{-10} \text{ kg}
$$

Resonant frequency:

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m^*}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{10^4}{1.028 \times 10^{-10}}}
$$

$$
f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{9.73 \times 10^{13}} = \frac{9.86 \times 10^6}{2\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_n \approx 1.57 \text{ MHz}}
$$

**(Marks split: 3 for k + 4 for f_n = 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B: (14 Marks)** [Polymer Materials, Properties & Applications]

**(a)** Compare the properties of **SU-8, PDMS, and Parylene-C** as MEMS materials. Discuss their typical applications in microfabrication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)?** Explain its significance in the design of polymer MEMS devices with at least two illustrative examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis of SU-8, PDMS, and Parylene-C:**

| Property | SU-8 | PDMS | Parylene-C |
|----------|------|------|------------|
| Type | Negative photoresist (thermoset) | Elastomer | Conformal CVD polymer |
| Young's Modulus | ~ 4 GPa | ~ 2 MPa | ~ 2.8 GPa |
| $T_g$ | > 200°C | −125°C | 80°C |
| Biocompatibility | Limited | Excellent | Excellent (USP VI) |
| Fabrication | Photolithography | Soft lithography molding | Chemical vapor deposition |
| Aspect Ratio | Up to 100:1 | Limited by mold | Conformal (no aspect limit) |
| Application | Microfluidic channels, bio-MEMS | Wearable sensors, soft robotics | Neural implant encapsulation |

**Applications:**
- **SU-8:** Used in high-aspect-ratio microstructures like **microneedle arrays** for drug delivery, **microfluidic channels** in lab-on-chip.
- **PDMS:** Used in **flexible biosensors** (e.g., skin-mounted ECG patches), **organ-on-chip** platforms.
- **Parylene-C:** Used as **conformal insulation** for neural probes and **hermetic sealing** of implantable devices.

**(Marks split: 4 for table + 3 for applications = 7 marks)**

**(b) Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):**

**Definition:** $T_g$ is the temperature at which a polymer transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state. Below $T_g$, polymer chains have restricted motion; above $T_g$, segmental motion becomes significant.

**Significance in Polymer MEMS:**
1. **Defines the upper temperature limit** for elastomeric operation (e.g., PDMS devices are limited to $T < 200$°C in molding).
2. **Affects mechanical properties** — modulus drops by 100–1000× above $T_g$.
3. **Critical for packaging** — SU-8 structures must be processed below its $T_g \approx 200$°C to avoid deformation.

**Examples:**
- **PDMS microfluidic chip:** Operating above $T_g$ (~ −125°C) is normal; however, autoclaving at 121°C for sterilization is permissible only briefly.
- **PMMA microstructures:** Cannot be used above 105°C without softening; unsuitable for autoclave sterilization in medical MEMS.

**(Marks split: 3 for definition + 2 for significance + 2 for examples = 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students often write $E$ in MPa instead of Pa, causing $f_n$ to be off by a factor of $10^6$. **Always convert to SI units first.**
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.24 factor:** In the effective mass formula, omitting 0.24 yields ~4× error in $f_n$.
> 3. **Confusing $T_g$ with melting point ($T_m$):** Thermosets like SU-8 do NOT melt — they degrade. $T_g$ for thermosets is the onset of softening, not flowing.
> 4. **Maxwell vs. Kelvin-Voigt confusion:** Maxwell = series (stress relaxation), Kelvin-Voigt = parallel (creep). Examiners award zero if reversed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Polymers** are macromolecular materials with $M_w > 10^4$ g/mol, essential second-class MEMS materials.
- [x] **SU-8** → negative photoresist, high aspect ratio, used in microfluidics and bio-MEMS.
- [x] **PDMS** → elastomer, biocompatible, used in soft lithography and wearable sensors.
- [x] **PMMA** → thermoplastic positive photoresist, used in X-ray lithography and lab-on-chip.
- [x] **Polyimide** → high $T_g$ (360°C), used in flexible circuits and aerospace.
- [x] **Parylene-C** → conformal CVD polymer, used for implantable device encapsulation.
- [x] **Viscoelasticity** is a defining polymer property — modeled by Maxwell (series) and Kelvin-Voigt (parallel).
- [x] **Maxwell stress relaxation:** $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ with $\tau = \eta/E$.
- [x] **Cantilever stiffness:** $k = E w t^3 / (4 L^3)$ — extremely thickness-dependent.
- [x] **Resonant frequency:** $f_n = \dfrac{t}{4\pi L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{0.24\,\rho}}$.
- [x] **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** defines the working thermal range and stiffness regime.
- [x] **Polymer advantages:** low cost, biocompatibility, flexibility, tunable chemistry.
- [x] **Polymer disadvantages:** low modulus, viscoelastic drift, temperature sensitivity, swelling in solvents.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Strategy:** Memorize the **2 viscoelastic models**, the **cantilever formulas (k & f_n)**, and the **4 polymer property tables**. These three cover ~80% of the marks in any Module 3 Polymers question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Microsystem Fabrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT632 - MICRO-ELECTRO-MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Microsystem Fabrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — OVERVIEW OF MICROSYSTEM FABRICATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Microsystem Fabrication** refers to the complete set of technological processes used to manufacture **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)** and micro-scale devices. It is a hybrid discipline that borrows heavily from **Integrated Circuit (IC) fabrication technology** while extending it with additional steps such as **bulk micromachining**, **surface micromachining**, and **non-silicon micromachining (LIGA)** to produce three-dimensional mechanical, optical, fluidic, and biomedical microstructures on a common substrate (typically **silicon**, glass, or polymers).

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a Tiny Skyscraper on a Wafer"

Imagine you are an **architect building a 100-storey micro-skyscraper** on a plot of land the size of a postage stamp. You cannot use bulldozers or cranes — you need **photons, ions, plasmas, and acids** as your tools.

| Real Construction | Microsystem Fabrication Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Surveying the land | **Wafer cleaning & preparation** (RCA cleaning) |
| Pouring the foundation | **Substrate (Silicon wafer)** selection |
| Drawing blueprints on the ground | **Photolithography** (UV light through a mask) |
| Removing earth to carve rooms | **Etching** (wet or dry) |
| Adding bricks/floors | **Thin-film deposition** (PVD, CVD) |
| Installing elevators & plumbing | **Doping, oxidation, ion implantation** |
| Welding two towers together | **Wafer bonding** (anodic, fusion, eutectic) |
| Painting & finishing | **Dicing, packaging, hermetic sealing** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Microsystem fabrication is fundamentally **additive (deposition)** and **subtractive (etching)** patterning of materials at the **micrometer (10⁻⁶ m)** and **sub-micrometer** scale, governed by the same physical chemistry principles used in semiconductor manufacturing — but extended to create **moving mechanical parts**, **cavities**, **beams**, **membranes**, and **channels** that interact with physical phenomena such as force, pressure, acceleration, and fluid flow.

### 1.2 Two Foundational Paradigms of MEMS Fabrication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every MEMS device is built using one (or a combination) of TWO paradigms:**
> 1. **IC-Based Silicon Micromachining** (dominant, ~85% of commercial MEMS) — uses **photolithography + etching + thin-film deposition** on silicon wafers.
> 2. **Non-IC / Non-Silicon Micromachining** — includes **LIGA** (X-ray lithography + electroplating), **polymers (SU-8, PDMS)**, **glass/silicon fusion bonding**, used when **high aspect-ratio** or **non-electrical** microstructures are required.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly used across all microsystem fabrication calculations:

- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $1 \, \text{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa}$
- **Wafer diameter standard:** **4-inch (100 mm), 6-inch (150 mm), 8-inch (200 mm), 12-inch (300 mm)**
- **Minimum feature size (modern MEMS):** **~0.18 µm** (advanced nodes) to **~100 µm** (typical MEMS)
- **Etch selectivity target:** $S \geq 10{:}1$ (mask-to-substrate)
- **Deposition rate typical range:** **1 nm/min to 1000 nm/min**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Process Flow of Microsystem Fabrication

Every microsystem, regardless of its end application (accelerometer, gyroscope, pressure sensor, micro-mirror, bio-chip), follows a **process-flow architecture** that is essentially a sequence of **pattern transfer operations**. The flow is layered, meaning each step builds on the previous one.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Generic Fabrication

1. **Substrate Selection & Preparation**
   - Choose a base wafer: **Silicon (100) or (111)**, **SOI (Silicon-On-Insulator)**, **glass (Pyrex 7740)**, **quartz**, **GaAs**, or **polymer (SU-8, PDMS)**.
   - Clean the wafer using **RCA cleaning** (a sequence of **RCA-1**: H₂O₂ + NH₄OH + H₂O to remove organics; **RCA-2**: H₂O₂ + HCl + H₂O to remove metallic contaminants).
   - **Why?** Any particle larger than the minimum feature size acts as a **killer defect** that ruins the entire device.

2. **Thin-Film Deposition (Add Material)**
   - Deposit a uniform layer of material (SiO₂, Si₃N₄, polysilicon, Al, Au, Cr, photoresist) over the entire wafer.
   - Techniques: **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD — evaporation, sputtering)**, **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD — LPCVD, PECVD)**.
   - **Why?** To create the functional or sacrificial layers needed later.

3. **Photolithography (Pattern Definition)**
   - Coat the wafer with **photoresist** (a light-sensitive polymer), soft-bake, expose through a **photomask**, develop.
   - **Why?** To transfer the 2-D geometric design from the mask onto the wafer.

4. **Etching (Subtract Material / Pattern Transfer)**
   - Remove material from regions not protected by the photoresist.
   - **Wet etching** (chemical, isotropic/anisotropic) vs **Dry etching** (RIE, DRIE, plasma).
   - **Why?** To physically carve out the desired microstructure.

5. **Doping / Ion Implantation (Modify Electrical Properties)**
   - Introduce **n-type** (P, As, Sb) or **p-type** (B, Ga) dopants into silicon.
   - **Why?** To create resistors, piezoresistors, conductive paths.

6. **Wafer Bonding (Integrate / Seal)**
   - Bond two wafers together (anodic bonding, fusion bonding, eutectic bonding).
   - **Why?** To create **cavities**, **hermetic seals**, **3-D structures**, and **multi-layer devices**.

7. **Dicing & Packaging**
   - Saw the wafer into individual **dies/chips**, then package (hermetic, ceramic, plastic).
   - **Why?** To protect the microstructure from the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — The Big Picture:**
> Module 4 emphasizes **photolithography, etching, deposition, bulk micromachining, surface micromachining, LIGA, and bonding**. These are the **seven pillars** of microsystem fabrication and form the basis of nearly all KTU university questions on this topic.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table contains every formula you must memorize for the KTU examination. All symbols and units are verified against standard MEMS/semiconductor physics references.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Etch Rate** | $R_{etch} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | $\text{nm/min}$ or $\mu\text{m/min}$ | Material thickness removed per unit time |
| 2 | **Selectivity** | $S = \dfrac{R_{etch, \, substrate}}{R_{etch, \, mask}}$ | Dimensionless | Ability of an etch to attack substrate faster than mask |
| 3 | **Anisotropy Factor** | $A_f = 1 - \dfrac{R_{lat}}{R_{vert}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | 1 = perfectly vertical; 0 = fully isotropic |
| 4 | **Deposition Rate (Evaporation)** | $R_{dep} = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $\text{nm/s}$ | Mass deposited per unit area per unit time |
| 5 | **Sputter Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Number of ejected atoms}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$ | Dimensionless | Efficiency of momentum-transfer sputtering |
| 6 | **Mean Free Path (PVD)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d^2 \, P}$ | $\text{m}$ | Average distance a vapor atom travels before collision |
| 7 | **Resist Spin-Coating Thickness** | $t \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\omega}}$ | $\mu\text{m}$ | Thinner resist at higher spin speed (rpm $\omega$) |
| 8 | **Rayleigh Resolution (Optical Lithography)** | $R = \dfrac{k_1 \, \lambda}{NA}$ | $\text{nm}$ | Minimum resolvable feature size |
| 9 | **Depth of Focus (DOF)** | $DOF = \dfrac{k_2 \, \lambda}{NA^2}$ | $\mu\text{m}$ | Tolerance to wafer non-flatness |
| 10 | **Process Latitude** | $\Delta E = \pm \, 10\% \text{ of nominal exposure dose}$ | $\text{mJ/cm}^2$ | Allowable exposure variation |
| 11 | **Etch Depth (Anisotropic Wet Etch, KOH on Si-100)** | $d = R_{(100)} \cdot t$ | $\mu\text{m}$ | Vertical etch rate × time |
| 12 | **Undercut (Isotropic Etch)** | $u = R_{lat} \cdot t$ | $\mu\text{m}$ | Lateral etch distance |
| 13 | **Oxide Growth (Deal-Grove Model)** | $x^2 + A x = B(t + \tau)$ | $\mu\text{m}$, minutes | Linear-parabolic oxide thickness vs time |
| 14 | **Oxide Growth Constant (Thermal)** | $B = \dfrac{2 D_{ox} \, C^*}{N_1}$ | $\mu\text{m}^2/\text{min}$ | Parabolic rate constant |
| 15 | **Lateral Etch (Surface Micromachining)** | $L_{release} = R_{etch, \, sacrificial} \cdot t$ | $\mu\text{m}$ | Released beam length after sacrificial etch |
| 16 | **Bonding Force (Anodic)** | $F = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A \, V^2}{2 d^2}$ | $\text{N}$ | Electrostatic force pulling glass to silicon |
| 17 | **Aspect Ratio (DRIE)** | $AR = \dfrac{\text{Depth}}{\text{Width}}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of etched depth to opening width |
| 18 | **LIGA Aspect Ratio** | $AR_{LIGA} \leq 100{:}1$ | Dimensionless | Extremely tall metallic microstructures |
| 19 | **Residual Stress in Thin Film** | $\sigma_{res} = \dfrac{E \, \Delta L / L}{1 - \nu}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Stoney's formula (simplified) |
| 20 | **Knudsen Number (Vacuum Regime)** | $K_n = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | Dimensionless | $\lambda$ = mean free path; $L$ = chamber dimension |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU university exams, formula-based numerical questions are **extremely common**. Memorize at minimum formulas 1, 2, 3, 8, 9, 11, 12, and 13 — these have appeared in nearly every MEMS paper of the last decade.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microsystem fabrication is not a purely academic exercise. It underpins devices that generate **billions of dollars** annually:

- **Automotive airbags** — ADXL-series MEMS accelerometers detect crash deceleration in **< 1 ms**.
- **Smartphones** — MEMS gyroscopes, accelerometers, microphones, and barometers are in **every** smartphone.
- **Inkjet printheads** — HP/Thermal uses **thermal** bubble ejection; Canon uses **piezoelectric** MEMS.
- **Medical implants** — Cochlear implants, neural probes, lab-on-chip diagnostics.
- **Optical switching** — Texas Instruments **DLP** chips use millions of movable micro-mirrors.
- **Pressure sensors** — Honeywell, Bosch, Infineon produce billions per year using **piezoresistive MEMS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **reliability and reproducibility** of microsystem fabrication — measured by **yield (%)** — is the single biggest economic factor. A 1% yield improvement on a 300 mm wafer can be worth **tens of millions of dollars per year** in revenue for a MEMS fab.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rayleigh Resolution in Optical Photolithography

The **Rayleigh criterion** sets the fundamental limit on how small a feature can be optically printed. It is derived from the diffraction of light through the lens aperture of the projection system.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

When a collimated beam of wavelength $\lambda$ passes through a circular aperture of diameter $D$, it produces an **Airy disk** diffraction pattern. The **half-angle** subtended by the first dark ring is:

$$\theta = 1.22 \, \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of the projection lens is defined as:

$$NA = n \sin \theta$$

For small angles (typical in lithography), $\sin \theta \approx \theta$, so:

$$NA \approx n \cdot \theta = 1.22 \, \frac{n \lambda}{D}$$

The **minimum resolvable linewidth** between two adjacent features is set by the requirement that the **central maxima of two Airy disks** are separated by at least the **Rayleigh distance**:

$$R = k_1 \, \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

where $k_1$ is a **process-dependent constant** (typically $0.4$ to $0.8$, with $0.61$ being the theoretical minimum). This is the fundamental **Resolution Equation** of optical lithography.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Why the formula takes this shape:**
> The numerator $\lambda$ shows that **shorter wavelengths print finer features** (which is why the industry moved from 436 nm → 365 nm → 248 nm → 193 nm → 13.5 nm EUV). The denominator $NA$ shows that **larger apertures focus light more tightly**, improving resolution.

### Step-by-Step Derivation — Depth of Focus (DOF)

If the wafer is not perfectly flat, the image plane shifts. The **Depth of Focus** is the maximum vertical tolerance before blurring occurs:

$$DOF = \pm \, \frac{\lambda}{2 \, NA^2}$$

In practice, with a process factor $k_2$:

$$DOF = k_2 \, \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}$$

where $k_2 \approx 1$ typically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Insight (KTU High-Yield):** Increasing $NA$ **halves DOF quadratically** while only **linearly improving resolution**. This is the classic **Resolution–DOF trade-off** and is a frequent KTU short-answer / 7-mark question topic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Etch Rate, Selectivity & Anisotropy

### Etch Rate Definition

Etch rate is the most fundamental process-monitoring metric. By definition:

$$R_{etch} = \frac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$$

where:
- $\Delta d$ = thickness of material removed (nm or µm)
- $\Delta t$ = duration of etching (min)

### Selectivity Derivation

Selectivity $S$ measures how much **faster** an etchant attacks the substrate than the masking layer:

$$S = \frac{R_{etch, \, substrate}}{R_{etch, \, mask}}$$

If the substrate etches at $1 \, \mu\text{m/min}$ and the mask at $0.01 \, \mu\text{m/min}$:

$$S = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$

### Anisotropy Factor Derivation

Anisotropy $A_f$ is derived from the ratio of **vertical** to **lateral** etch rates:

$$A_f = 1 - \frac{R_{lat}}{R_{vert}}$$

For perfectly anisotropic etching: $R_{lat} = 0 \Rightarrow A_f = 1$.
For purely isotropic etching: $R_{lat} = R_{vert} \Rightarrow A_f = 0$.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A silicon wafer is etched in KOH at $60°\text{C}$. The vertical etch rate along the (100) plane is $1.4 \, \mu\text{m/min}$. The lateral etch rate along the (111) plane is $0.005 \, \mu\text{m/min}$. The SiO₂ mask etches at $0.014 \, \mu\text{m/min}$.

**Find:** (i) Anisotropy factor, (ii) Etch depth after 30 minutes, (iii) Selectivity of Si over SiO₂.

**Solution:**

(i) Anisotropy:
$$A_f = 1 - \frac{R_{lat}}{R_{vert}} = 1 - \frac{0.005}{1.4} = 1 - 0.00357 \approx 0.996$$

(ii) Etch depth after 30 minutes:
$$d = R_{(100)} \cdot t = 1.4 \, \frac{\mu\text{m}}{\text{min}} \times 30 \, \text{min} = 42 \, \mu\text{m}$$

(iii) Selectivity of Si over SiO₂:
$$S = \frac{R_{Si}}{R_{SiO_2}} = \frac{1.4}{0.014} = 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The very high selectivity ($S = 100$) means a thin SiO₂ mask (~420 nm) is enough to protect the silicon for 30 minutes of KOH etching — this is why SiO₂ is the standard mask for anisotropic bulk micromachining of silicon in KOH/TMAH.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deal–Grove Model for Thermal Oxidation

The **Deal–Grove model** (1965) describes the growth of SiO₂ on silicon at high temperature (typically 800–1200°C). It is one of the most important formulas in semiconductor fabrication.

### Full Derivation

The model assumes that oxidation is governed by **three sequential fluxes**:

1. **Flux of oxidant** from gas phase to the oxide surface ($F_1$).
2. **Flux of oxidant** diffusing through the existing oxide ($F_2$).
3. **Flux of oxidant** reacting with silicon at the Si/SiO₂ interface ($F_3$).

In **steady state**, these fluxes are equal: $F_1 = F_2 = F_3$.

Solving the diffusion equation through the oxide of thickness $x$, with concentration $C^*$ at the outer surface and $C_i$ at the Si interface:

$$F = \frac{D_{ox} \, (C^* - C_i)}{x}$$

The interface reaction rate gives:

$$F = k_s \, C_i$$

Combining and eliminating $C_i$, the differential equation for oxide growth is:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{B}{2x + A}$$

where:
- $A = 2 D_{ox} / k_s$ (linear rate constant, in µm)
- $B = 2 D_{ox} C^* / N_1$ (parabolic rate constant, in $\mu\text{m}^2/\text{min}$)
- $N_1$ = number of oxidant molecules per unit volume of SiO₂ ($\approx 2.2 \times 10^{22} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$ for O₂)

Integrating from initial oxide thickness $x_0$ at $t = 0$ gives:

$$x^2 + A x = B (t + \tau)$$

where $\tau = (x_0^2 + A x_0) / B$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Two limiting regimes:**
> - For **thick oxides** ($x \gg A$): $x^2 \approx B t \Rightarrow$ **parabolic growth** (diffusion-limited).
> - For **thin oxides** ($x \ll A$): $A x \approx B t \Rightarrow$ **linear growth** (reaction-limited).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Lateral Etch in Sacrificial Release (Surface Micromachining)

In **surface micromachining**, a sacrificial layer (e.g., SiO₂) is deposited between the substrate and the structural layer (e.g., polysilicon). The sacrificial layer is later etched away in **HF**, releasing the moving structure.

The lateral etch depth (the distance the HF has to travel from the etch hole to release a beam) is:

$$L_{release} = R_{HF, \, SiO_2} \cdot t_{release}$$

where $R_{HF, \, SiO_2}$ is the lateral etch rate of SiO₂ in HF (typically $1$–$2 \, \mu\text{m/min}$).

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A polysilicon micro-beam sits on top of a $2 \, \mu\text{m}$ thick SiO₂ sacrificial layer. HF etches through $5 \, \mu\text{m} \times 5 \, \mu\text{m}$ etch holes spaced $200 \, \mu\text{m}$ apart along the beam. The HF lateral etch rate is $1.5 \, \mu\text{m/min}$.

**Find:** Time to fully release the beam.

**Solution:**
$$t_{release} = \frac{L_{max}}{R_{HF}} = \frac{200/2}{1.5} = \frac{100}{1.5} \approx 66.7 \, \text{min}$$

The etch hole is $200 \, \mu\text{m}$ from the center, so HF must travel **100 µm laterally** from the nearest hole. The release time is therefore approximately **66.7 minutes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Smaller etch holes mean more uniform release, but they also restrict the HF flow and **increase** release time. Designers must balance etch-hole size, spacing, and process time.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Anodic Bonding Electrostatic Force

In **anodic (field-assisted) bonding**, a glass wafer (Pyrex 7740) is bonded to silicon at ~400°C and ~1000 V. The glass contains Na⁺ ions that drift toward the cathode, leaving a **depletion layer** of negative charge at the glass-silicon interface. The induced electrostatic force is:

$$F = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A \, V^2}{2 d^2}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$ (vacuum permittivity)
- $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.6$ for Pyrex glass (relative permittivity)
- $A$ = bonding area (m²)
- $V$ = applied voltage (V)
- $d$ = depletion layer thickness (~1 µm)

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Anodic bonding at $V = 1000 \, \text{V}$ on a 100 mm wafer ($A = \pi \times (0.05)^2 = 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$), with $d = 1 \, \mu\text{m}$ and $\varepsilon_r = 4.6$.

**Find:** Electrostatic bonding force.

**Solution:**

$$F = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 4.6 \times (7.85 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1000)^2}{2 \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

Step 1 — Compute numerator:
$$\text{Numerator} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4.6 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6$$
$$= 8.854 \times 4.6 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-12-3+6} = 8.854 \times 4.6 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-9}$$
$$= 319.6 \times 10^{-9} = 3.196 \times 10^{-7}$$

Step 2 — Denominator:
$$2 \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2 \times 10^{-12}$$

Step 3 — Force:
$$F = \frac{3.196 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.598 \times 10^{5} \, \text{N}$$

Step 4 — Pressure:
$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{1.598 \times 10^5}{7.85 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.04 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} \approx 200 \, \text{atm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** A **200 atm** electrostatic pressure at only 1000 V is what makes anodic bonding so effective — it pulls the two wafers into intimate contact, allowing Si–O chemical bonds to form permanently.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Fabrication Techniques

| Technique | Material Removed/Added | Direction | Selectivity | Aspect Ratio | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **KOH Wet Etch (Si-100)** | Si | Anisotropic (54.74°) | Very High (~100) | Moderate (1:1 to 10:1) | Bulk micromachining, V-grooves, membranes |
| **TMAH Wet Etch** | Si | Anisotropic | High (~50) | Moderate | CMOS-compatible bulk micromachining |
| **Isotropic HNA Etch** | Si | Isotropic | Low | Low | Pre-thinning, smoothing |
| **RIE (Reactive Ion Etch)** | Si, SiO₂, polymer | Anisotropic (slightly) | Moderate (5–20) | Moderate (5:1) | General pattern transfer |
| **DRIE (Bosch Process)** | Si | Highly Anisotropic | High | **Very High (50:1)** | Deep trenches, through-wafer vias |
| **HF Wet Etch** | SiO₂ | Isotropic | Infinite vs Si | Low | Sacrificial release, oxide patterning |
| **LPCVD Poly-Si** | Adds poly-Si | Conformal | N/A | N/A | Structural layers in surface micromachining |
| **PECVD Si₃N₄** | Adds nitride | Conformal | N/A | N/A | Masking layer, dielectric |
| **Evaporation (PVD)** | Adds metal | Line-of-sight | N/A | N/A | Electrodes, bond pads |
| **Sputtering (PVD)** | Adds metal | Quasi-conformal | N/A | N/A | Adhesion layers, refractory metals |
| **LIGA** | Adds metal via plating | Highly anisotropic | Very High | **Ultra-high (100:1)** | X-ray optics, micro-gears, metallic microstructures |
| **Anodic Bonding** | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | Glass–Si sealing |
| **Fusion Bonding** | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | Si–Si or Si–glass, SOI wafers |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Master Process Flow of Microsystem Fabrication

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Wafer Selection] --> B[Wafer Cleaning RCA-1 & RCA-2]
    B --> C[Thin Film Deposition]
    C --> D[Photoresist Coating & Soft Bake]
    D --> E[Mask Alignment & UV Exposure]
    E --> F[Photoresist Development]
    F --> G{Etching Step}
    G --> G1[Wet Etching KOH / TMAH / HF]
    G --> G2[Dry Etching RIE / DRIE / Plasma]
    G1 --> H[Photoresist Stripping]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Doping / Ion Implantation]
    I --> J[Thermal Oxidation]
    J --> K{Wafer Bonding?}
    K --> K1[Anodic Bonding]
    K --> K2[Fusion Bonding]
    K --> K3[Eutectic Bonding]
    K1 --> L[Dicing]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[Packaging Hermetic / Ceramic]
    M --> N[Final MEMS Device]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic MEMS Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Physical Signal Input Pressure / Acceleration / Force] --> S2[Microsensor Element Piezoresistor / Capacitor]
    S2 --> S3[Signal Conditioning ASIC]
    S3 --> S4[ADC]
    S4 --> S5[Digital Output SPI / I2C / Analog]
    S5 --> S6[Microsystem Processor / User]

    F1[Fabrication Layer 1 Silicon Substrate] -.supports.-> S2
    F2[Fabrication Layer 2 Sacrificial Oxide] -.enables release.-> S2
    F3[Fabrication Layer 3 Structural Polysilicon] -.forms beam.-> S2
    F4[Fabrication Layer 4 Metal Electrodes] -.electrical readout.-> S3
    F5[Fabrication Layer 5 Bonded Glass Cap] -.hermetic seal.-> S2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Photolithography Step Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Step 1: Wafer Preparation & Dehydration Bake] --> L2[Step 2: HMDS Vapor Priming]
    L2 --> L3[Step 3: Photoresist Spin Coat]
    L3 --> L4[Step 4: Soft Bake 90-100 C]
    L4 --> L5[Step 5: Mask Alignment]
    L5 --> L6[Step 6: UV Exposure I-line 365 nm / DUV 248 nm]
    L6 --> L7[Step 7: Post Exposure Bake]
    L7 --> L8[Step 8: Development in TMAH or AZ Developer]
    L8 --> L9[Step 9: Hard Bake 120-180 C]
    L9 --> L10[Step 10: Pattern Transfer Etching]
    L10 --> L11[Step 11: Photoresist Stripping in Acetone or NMP]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Bulk vs Surface Micromachining Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BULK[Bulk Micromachining]
        B1[Silicon Wafer] --> B2[Deposit Mask SiO2 or Si3N4]
        B2 --> B3[Photolithography]
        B3 --> B4[Anisotropic Etch KOH / TMAH]
        B4 --> B5[3-D Structure Membrane / Cantilever / V-groove]
    end

    subgraph SURFACE[Surface Micromachining]
        S1[Silicon Wafer] --> S2[Deposit Sacrificial Layer SiO2]
        S2 --> S3[Deposit Structural Layer Polysilicon]
        S3 --> S4[Photolithography & Pattern Structural Layer]
        S4 --> S5[Sacrificial Etch in HF]
        S5 --> S6[Released Moving Structure Beam / Plate]
    end

    BULK -.contrast.-> SURFACE
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — LIGA Process Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step A: Metallic Substrate Preparation] --> P2[Step B: Spin Coat Thick PMMA Resist]
    P2 --> P3[Step C: Synchrotron X-Ray Exposure Through Mask]
    P3 --> P4[Step D: Resist Development Removes Exposed Regions]
    P4 --> P5[Step E: Electroplating Fills Cavity With Metal Ni / Cu / Au]
    P5 --> P6[Step F: Residual PMMA Stripping]
    P6 --> P7[Step G: Final Metallic High Aspect Ratio Microstructure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These schematics represent the **process architecture** of microsystem fabrication. In actual lab/fab environments, each step has multiple sub-steps and quality-control checkpoints (e.g., ellipsometry for film thickness, SEM for critical dimensions, profilometry for etch depth).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Instructions:** Answer in **3–4 sentences maximum**. Direct, to-the-point factual answers score best. Avoid irrelevant elaboration.

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define microsystem fabrication. List the major steps involved.**

**Model Answer:**

> Microsystem fabrication is the set of processes used to manufacture MEMS devices that integrate mechanical, electrical, and often optical or fluidic functions on a single substrate (typically silicon). The major steps are: (i) substrate selection and cleaning, (ii) thin-film deposition, (iii) photolithography, (iv) etching (wet or dry), (v) doping/oxidation, (vi) wafer bonding, and (vii) dicing and packaging. Each step builds upon the previous one in a layered, sequential process flow.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**What is selectivity in etching? Why is high selectivity important in microsystem fabrication?**

**Model Answer:**

> Selectivity $S$ is the ratio of the etch rate of the substrate material to the etch rate of the masking material:
>
> $$S = \frac{R_{etch, \, substrate}}{R_{etch, \, mask}}$$
>
> High selectivity is critical because it allows a thin masking layer to protect the underlying material for long etch durations. Without high selectivity, the mask would be consumed before the desired etch depth is achieved, causing pattern failure and device malfunction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split as **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**. Choose **either A or B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the photolithography process used in microsystem fabrication. List the major steps and explain the role of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Photolithography is the technique of transferring geometric patterns from a photomask onto a substrate coated with a light-sensitive **photoresist**. It is the cornerstone of microsystem fabrication because it enables precise, repeatable patterning at the micrometer and sub-micrometer scale.

The major steps are:

1. **Wafer Preparation & Dehydration Bake** — Removes moisture from the wafer surface (~150–200°C) to ensure good photoresist adhesion.
2. **HMDS Priming** — Hexamethyldisilazane vapor is deposited to make the surface hydrophobic, promoting uniform resist wetting.
3. **Photoresist Spin Coating** — Liquid resist (e.g., AZ-5214, S-1813) is dispensed and spun at 1000–6000 rpm to form a uniform thin film. The thickness follows approximately $t \propto 1/\sqrt{\omega}$.
4. **Soft Bake** — Hot plate at 90–100°C for ~60 s to evaporate solvent and densify the film.
5. **Mask Alignment & UV Exposure** — The wafer is aligned to the photomask, then exposed to UV light (typically I-line at 365 nm or DUV at 248 nm) in a stepper or contact aligner. The exposure dose is typically 50–200 mJ/cm².
6. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** — Reduces standing-wave effects caused by substrate reflection.
7. **Development** — For positive resist, exposed regions dissolve in developer (TMAH-based, e.g., AZ-300); for negative resist, unexposed regions dissolve.
8. **Hard Bake** — 120–180°C to harden the remaining resist before etching.
9. **Pattern Transfer** — The developed pattern is transferred into the underlying film via etching.
10. **Resist Stripping** — Photoresist is removed in acetone, NMP, or O₂ plasma ashing.

The **minimum resolvable feature size** is governed by the Rayleigh equation:
$$R = k_1 \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the various wet etching techniques used in silicon micromachining. Compare isotropic and anisotropic etching.**

**Model Answer:**

**Wet etching** uses liquid chemicals to remove material. Silicon wet etching uses alkaline solutions such as **KOH (potassium hydroxide)**, **TMAH (tetramethylammonium hydroxide)**, **EDP (ethylenediamine pyrocatechol)**, or **CsOH**. SiO₂ is etched in **HF (hydrofluoric acid)**, and aluminum is etched in **phosphoric acid**.

**Isotropic Etching:**
- Etches in **all directions at the same rate** ($R_{vert} = R_{lat}$).
- Example: **HNA** (Hydrofluoric–Nitric–Acetic) etch for silicon; HF for SiO₂.
- Used for: surface cleaning, sacrificial release, pre-thinning.
- **Anisotropy factor** $A_f = 0$.

**Anisotropic Etching:**
- Etches **different crystal planes at different rates**.
- Example: **KOH (30%, 80°C)** etches Si(100) at ~1.4 µm/min and Si(111) at only ~0.005 µm/min, producing a **selectivity of ~400**.
- The exposed planes on (100) silicon form **54.74° sidewalls**, creating V-grooves, pyramids, and membranes.
- **Anisotropy factor** $A_f \approx 0.996$ (almost 1).

| Parameter | Isotropic | Anisotropic |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | All directions | Crystal-plane dependent |
| Undercut | Severe | Minimal |
| Mask requirement | Conformal | Pre-shaped openings |
| Aspect ratio | Low | High |
| Typical etchant | HNA, HF | KOH, TMAH, EDP |

**Comparison:** Anisotropic etching is preferred for MEMS because it produces well-defined geometric features with vertical or sloped sidewalls, ideal for cantilevers, membranes, and V-grooves. Isotropic etching is used when conformal release (e.g., surface micromachining) or smooth surface finishing is required.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the surface micromachining process in detail with a suitable example. How does it differ from bulk micromachining?**

**Model Answer:**

**Surface micromachining** is the process of building MEMS structures by **depositing and patterning thin films** on the surface of a substrate, then using a **sacrificial layer** that is selectively removed to release moving mechanical structures.

**Process Flow (Example: Polysilicon cantilever over SiO₂):**

1. **Substrate preparation** — RCA-cleaned silicon wafer.
2. **Sacrificial layer deposition** — LPCVD SiO₂ (~2 µm).
3. **Anchors patterning** — Photolithography and etching to define anchor regions where the structural layer will be fixed to the substrate.
4. **Structural layer deposition** — LPCVD polysilicon (~2 µm).
5. **Structural layer patterning** — Photolithography and RIE to define the beam shape.
6. **Sacrificial etch (release)** — Immersion in **HF** removes the SiO₂, releasing the beam.
7. **Critical-point drying** — Prevents **stiction** between the beam and substrate.

**Difference from Bulk Micromachining:**

| Aspect | Surface | Bulk |
|---|---|---|
| Material removed | Thin sacrificial layer | The wafer itself |
| Structure thickness | 1–10 µm | 10–500 µm |
| Wafer consumption | Minimal | Significant |
| Released structures | Beams, plates, membranes | V-grooves, cavities, membranes |
| Complexity | Multi-layer process flow | Single-crystal etching |
| Stiction risk | High | Low |
| Material flexibility | Poly-Si, SiN, metals | Single-crystal Si |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the LIGA process. What are its advantages and limitations compared to silicon micromachining?**

**Model Answer:**

**LIGA** is the German acronym for **Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung** (X-ray lithography, electroplating, molding). It produces **ultra-high aspect ratio** metallic microstructures.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Substrate preparation** — A conductive substrate (e.g., Cu, Au-coated Si) is prepared.
2. **PMMA resist coating** — A thick layer (typically **100 µm to several millimeters**) of polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) is deposited by spin coating or lamination.
3. **X-ray exposure** — **Synchrotron radiation** (λ ≈ 0.2–1 nm) is passed through a special **X-ray mask** (made of Au absorber on a SiC or Si membrane). The high-energy X-rays penetrate the thick PMMA, exposing the entire depth in one shot.
4. **Development** — Exposed PMMA is dissolved in GG developer, leaving a deep negative-tone mold.
5. **Electroplating** — Metal (Ni, Cu, Au, Fe-Ni alloy) is electrodeposited into the cavities, filling from the substrate upward.
6. **PMMA stripping** — Residual PMMA is removed in acetone or NMP.
7. **Optional molding step (Abformung)** — The metal master can be used as a mold insert for hot embossing of polymer replicas, enabling mass production.

**Advantages:**

- **Extreme aspect ratios** (up to 100:1) — far beyond DRIE.
- **Tall microstructures** (hundreds of µm to mm scale).
- **Excellent sidewall verticality** ($A_f > 0.99$).
- **Wide material choice** (Ni, Cu, Au, Permalloy, even ceramics via slip casting).
- **Sub-micrometer lateral resolution** due to short-wavelength X-rays.

**Limitations:**

- **Synchrotron radiation** is extremely expensive and not universally accessible.
- **X-ray masks** are costly and difficult to fabricate.
- **Slow throughput** — not suited for high-volume consumer MEMS.
- **Limited material compatibility** with standard IC processes.

**Comparison with Silicon Micromachining:** LIGA excels where silicon micromachining fails — in producing **tall, metallic, high-aspect-ratio** structures for micro-actuators, X-ray optics, micro-fluidics channels, and micro-gears. For standard MEMS (accelerometers, gyroscopes), silicon micromachining is far cheaper and more compatible with IC integration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Don't** confuse **selectivity** with **anisotropy**. Selectivity = mask vs substrate etch rate. Anisotropy = vertical vs lateral etch rate. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> - **Don't** write the Rayleigh formula as $R = \lambda / NA$. The correct form is $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$. Forgetting $k_1$ loses **2 marks**.
> - **Don't** claim "KOH etches Si(100) faster than Si(111)" — that's correct, but **always quantify** it: $1.4 \, \mu\text{m/min}$ vs $0.005 \, \mu\text{m/min}$ — examiners reward numerical precision.
> - **Don't** list "LIGA" steps without mentioning **synchrotron X-rays** — this is the defining feature.
> - **Don't** forget that **stiction** is a critical failure mechanism in surface micromachining — always mention **critical-point drying**.
> - For **14-mark answers**, **draw diagrams** wherever possible. A clearly labelled figure can earn 1–2 bonus marks.
> - Always end numerical problems with **units** and a **physical interpretation sentence** (e.g., "This corresponds to an aspect ratio of 50:1, suitable for DRIE applications").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Overview of Microsystem Fabrication**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Memorize
- [ ] Microsystem fabrication = sequence of **additive (deposition)** and **subtractive (etching)** processes on silicon/glass substrates.
- [ ] Two paradigms: **Bulk micromachining** vs **Surface micromachining**.
- [ ] LIGA = **X-ray lithography + electroplating + molding** for ultra-high aspect ratio metallic parts.
- [ ] Wafer bonding has **three main flavors**: anodic, fusion, eutectic.
- [ ] Photolithography resolution: $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$ — shorter λ and higher NA give finer features.
- [ ] DOF: $DOF = k_2 \lambda / NA^2$ — increasing NA quadratically reduces focus depth.
- [ ] Etch rate: $R_{etch} = \Delta d / \Delta t$.
- [ ] Selectivity: $S = R_{substrate} / R_{mask}$.
- [ ] Anisotropy factor: $A_f = 1 - R_{lat}/R_{vert}$ → 1 = perfect vertical, 0 = isotropic.
- [ ] KOH on Si(100): $R \approx 1.4 \, \mu\text{m/min}$; selectivity vs SiO₂ $\approx$ 100–400.
- [ ] HF etches SiO₂ but **not silicon** — perfect for sacrificial release.
- [ ] DRIE (Bosch process) achieves aspect ratios of **50:1** with scallop-free sidewalls.
- [ ] Deal–Grove model: $x^2 + Ax = B(t + \tau)$ — parabolic for thick oxides, linear for thin.
- [ ] Anodic bonding uses **Na⁺ depletion** in Pyrex glass at ~400°C and ~1000 V.

### ✅ Numerical / Formula Hotspots for the Exam
- [ ] Etch depth vs time problems.
- [ ] Selectivity and anisotropy calculation.
- [ ] Rayleigh resolution with given $\lambda$, NA, $k_1$.
- [ ] DOF for given process conditions.
- [ ] Deal–Grove oxide thickness growth.
- [ ] Release time for surface micromachined beams.
- [ ] Electrostatic force in anodic bonding.

### ✅ Process Selection Rules (Common KTU Question)
- [ ] Need **high aspect ratio + deep trenches** → **DRIE**.
- [ ] Need **sloped sidewalls (54.74°)** → **KOH/TMAH on (100) Si**.
- [ ] Need **released moving structures** → **Surface micromachining + HF release**.
- [ ] Need **metallic tall microstructures** → **LIGA**.
- [ ] Need **CMOS-compatible** bulk etching → **TMAH** (no Na⁺ or K⁺ contamination).
- [ ] Need **glass–Si bonding** → **Anodic bonding**.
- [ ] Need **ultra-clean Si–Si bonding** → **Fusion bonding**.

### ✅ Common Defects & Failure Modes (Examiner Loves These)
- [ ] **Stiction** in surface micromachining — use critical-point drying or freeze-drying.
- [ ] **Notching** in DRIE — caused by charge accumulation at oxide interfaces.
- [ ] **Hillocks** in evaporated Al films — stress-induced protrusions.
- [ ] **Standing waves** in photoresist — suppress with bottom anti-reflective coating (BARC) and PEB.
- [ ] **Stress-induced curling** of thin-film cantilevers — minimize by controlling residual stress.

### ✅ One-Line Exam-Ready Definitions
- **Microsystem Fabrication:** The complete set of lithographic, deposition, etching, bonding, and packaging processes used to manufacture MEMS devices.
- **Photolithography:** Optical pattern transfer from a mask to a photoresist-coated wafer.
- **Bulk Micromachining:** Subtractive patterning of the silicon wafer itself using anisotropic etchants.
- **Surface Micromachining:** Additive patterning using deposited thin films and sacrificial layer release.
- **LIGA:** High-aspect-ratio metallic microfabrication using X-ray lithography and electroplating.
- **DRIE:** Deep Reactive Ion Etching using the time-multiplexed Bosch process.
- **Selectivity:** Ratio of etch rates of target material to masking material.
- **Anisotropy:** Degree of directional preference in etching.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Overview of Microsystem Fabrication**

topics.title: Photolithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT632 - MICRO-ELECTRO-MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Photolithography # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Photolithography** is a microfabrication process used in MEMS and semiconductor manufacturing to selectively pattern thin films (or bulk substrates) by transferring a geometric shape from a *photomask* onto a photosensitive chemical layer — called a **photoresist** — coated on the substrate. The patterned photoresist then acts as a protective mask during subsequent etching, doping, deposition, or lift-off processes. The word originates from the Greek: *photo* (light), *lithos* (stone), and *graphein* (to write) — literally, **"writing with light on stone."** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Photolithography is a **core process module** in the MEMS fabrication unit. Students must be able to describe the **step sequence**, classify the **exposure modes**, distinguish between **positive and negative photoresists**, and apply the **resolution equation** to compute the minimum feature size. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **screen-printing artist** who wants to print a logo onto a T-shirt. You take a stencil (the *mask*), lay it on the fabric, spray paint through the openings, and remove the stencil to reveal the pattern. Photolithography is the **nanoscale, light-driven equivalent**: - The **T-shirt** = the silicon wafer (or substrate). - The **stencil** = the photomask (a quartz plate with chromium patterns). - The **spray paint** = ultraviolet (UV) light. - The **fabric's reaction to the dye** = the photoresist's chemical change upon UV exposure. After the "light spray," instead of drying paint, the wafer is rinsed in a **developer solution** (like water revealing invisible ink), and the *exposed* or *unexposed* regions dissolve, leaving a precise, microscale pattern that protects the underlying material during the next processing step. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$ > - **Typical UV exposure wavelength (g-line):** $\lambda = 436 \, \text{nm}$ > - **Typical UV exposure wavelength (i-line):** $\lambda = 365 \, \text{nm}$ > - **Deep UV (DUV) wavelength:** $\lambda = 193 \, \text{nm}$ (ArF excimer laser) > - **Extreme UV (EUV) wavelength:** $\lambda = 13.5 \, \text{nm}$ > - **Photoresist film thickness range:** **0.5 µm to 2 µm** > - **Spin coating rotational speed range:** **1,000 rpm to 6,000 rpm** > - **Soft-bake temperature range:** **90 °C to 120 °C** > - **Hard-bake temperature range:** **120 °C to 180 °C** ## 1.4 Why Photolithography is Critical to MEMS Photolithography is the **enabling technology** of all integrated microsystems. Without it, we could not build: - **MEMS accelerometers** (airbag sensors). - **Gyroscopes** in smartphones. - **Pressure sensors** in biomedical implants. - **Inkjet printer nozzles** (thermal bubble jets). - **Microfluidic channels** for lab-on-chip devices. The *resolution* of the process directly determines the **smallest mechanical feature** (e.g., beam width, gap spacing, membrane thickness) that can be fabricated, which in turn dictates the sensitivity, frequency response, and functionality of the MEMS device. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Eight Canonical Steps of Photolithography The complete photolithographic sequence is a **linear pipeline** of eight discrete operations. Each step must be executed with extreme precision; a single contamination or timing error can destroy an entire wafer batch. ### Step 1: Wafer Cleaning - **Objective:** Remove organic residues, native oxides, and particulate contaminants. - **Typical reagents:** Piranha solution ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2$), RCA-1 ($\text{NH}_4\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$). - **Why it matters:** Contaminants cause **pinholes** in the resist, leading to etch defects. ### Step 2: Dehydration Bake - **Objective:** Drive off adsorbed moisture from the wafer surface. - **Temperature:** 150 °C – 200 °C for ~5–10 minutes. - **Why it matters:** Moisture prevents uniform resist adhesion (poor **wettability**). ### Step 3: Priming (Adhesion Promotion) - **Objective:** Apply a thin layer of **Hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS)** to promote photoresist adhesion to the wafer. - **Mechanism:** HMDS reacts with surface —OH groups, forming a hydrophobic Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃ monolayer. - **Why it matters:** Without HMDS, the aqueous developer can **undercut** the resist pattern, destroying fine features. ### Step 4: Photoresist Coating (Spin Coating) - **Objective:** Deposit a uniform, thin, defect-free photoresist film. - **Method:** A measured volume of liquid resist is dispensed onto the wafer, which is then spun at high speed. Centrifugal force spreads the resist, and viscous shear thins it to the desired thickness. - **Film thickness equation:** $$ t = \frac{K \cdot C^{\beta} \cdot \eta^{\gamma}}{\omega^{\alpha}} $$ where: - $t$ = final film thickness - $K$ = empirical constant - $C$ = resist solid content (mass fraction) - $\eta$ = resist viscosity - $\omega$ = angular spin speed - $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = empirically fitted exponents (typically $\alpha \approx 0.5$) ### Step 5: Soft Bake (Pre-Exposure Bake) - **Objective:** Evaporate residual solvent from the resist film, improving adhesion and mechanical stability. - **Temperature:** 90 °C – 120 °C for ~60 s on a hot plate (or 30 min in a convection oven). - **Risk:** Over-baking causes **resist reticulation** (cracking) and reduces photosensitivity. ### Step 6: Alignment and Exposure - **Objective:** Align the photomask to existing wafer features and selectively expose the resist to UV light. - **Critical parameters:** Exposure dose (mJ/cm²), exposure time, and the **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of the projection optics. - **Exposure modes** (see Section 2.3). ### Step 7: Post-Exposure Bake (PEB) - **Objective:** Drive the photo-induced chemical reaction to completion, reducing standing-wave effects in the resist. - **Temperature:** 100 °C – 130 °C for ~60 s. - **Why it matters:** Smooths sidewall profiles, improving **critical dimension (CD) control**. ### Step 8: Development - **Objective:** Dissolve either the exposed regions (positive resist) or unexposed regions (negative resist) using a developer solution. - **Typical developer:** Tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAH, 0.26 N) for positive resists. - **Outcome:** A 3-D patterned resist structure on the wafer. ### Step 9 (Optional): Hard Bake - **Objective:** Further harden the developed resist to improve its etch resistance. - **Temperature:** 120 °C – 180 °C. - **Note:** Excessive hard-bake can cause resist to flow, distorting patterns. --- ## 2.2 Photoresist Chemistry: Positive vs. Negative | Property | **Positive Photoresist** | **Negative Photoresist** | |---|---|---| | **Chemical change upon UV exposure** | Polymer chain **scission** (breaking) | Polymer chain **cross-linking** | | **Solubility after exposure** | Exposed regions become **soluble** (dissolve in developer) | Exposed regions become **insoluble** (hardened) | | **Pattern transferred** | Same as the mask | Inverse of the mask | | **Resolution** | **High** (no swelling) | **Lower** (resist swells in developer) | | **Common examples** | AZ-5214E, S1813, PMMA | SU-8, NR7-1500P, KTFR | | **Etch resistance** | Moderate (poor for dry etch) | **Excellent** (SU-8 is iconic for thick MEMS) | | **Typical thickness** | 0.5 – 2 µm | 1 – 200 µm (SU-8 enables tall structures) | > [!NOTE] > **SU-8** is a particularly important negative photoresist in MEMS because it can be spun to thicknesses > 100 µm and patterned into **high-aspect-ratio** structures (e.g., microchannels, comb drives). It is a near-UV, epoxy-based, cross-linking resist. --- ## 2.3 Exposure Modes: Contact, Proximity, and Projection | Mode | Description | Resolution | Mask Wear | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Contact** | Mask physically touches the resist-coated wafer. | ~0.5 µm | **Severe** (mask degrades quickly) | Lab/research | | **Proximity** | Mask held a small gap (10 – 50 µm) above the wafer. | ~2 – 5 µm | Minimal | Older production | | **Projection** | Mask imaged onto wafer via **high-NA lens system**. | **< 0.1 µm** (modern DUV/EUV) | None (mask isolated) | **Industry standard** | > [!IMPORTANT] > Modern MEMS and IC production universally uses **step-and-scan projection** systems. The mask is *never* in contact with the wafer, eliminating mask damage and contamination. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W_{\text{min}} = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}$ | Minimum printable half-pitch (Rayleigh) | m | | 2 | $\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2}$ | Depth of focus | m | | 3 | $t = \dfrac{K \cdot C^{\beta} \cdot \eta^{\gamma}}{\omega^{\alpha}}$ | Spin-coat film thickness | m | | 4 | $\text{Exposure Dose} = I \cdot t_{\text{exp}}$ | Energy per unit area | mJ/cm² | | 5 | $\text{Resistivity} = \dfrac{E_{\text{film}}}{I_{\text{probe}}}$ | Sheet resistance of thin films | Ω/sq | | 6 | $A_R = \dfrac{H}{W}$ | Aspect ratio of resist feature | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{CD Control} = \pm 3\sigma$ | Critical dimension variation | nm | | 8 | $\text{MTF} = \dfrac{\sin(2\pi f)}{2\pi f}$ | Modulation Transfer Function (idealized) | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > In Equation 1 and 2, $k_1$ and $k_2$ are **process-dependent factors** ($k_1 \approx 0.4$ for modern immersion lithography, $k_2 \approx 1$). $\lambda$ is the exposure wavelength and **NA** is the numerical aperture of the lens. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Photolithography's primary engineering utility is its ability to **batch-fabricate millions of identical microscale features simultaneously** on a single 200 mm or 300 mm wafer, driving the cost per die to a fraction of a cent. In MEMS: - **Resist patterning defines the geometry of mechanical structures** (cantilevers, membranes, gears, channels). - **Lift-off processes** use patterned resist as a *sacrificial stencil* for metal deposition, producing fine metal electrodes. - **Etch masks** protect underlying material from plasma or wet chemical etchants. - **Bonding alignment** marks patterned by lithography enable wafer-level packaging of MEMS dies. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Rayleigh Resolution Limit for Photolithography The **minimum resolvable feature size** $W_{\text{min}}$ in a projection lithography system is governed by **diffraction** of light through the projection lens aperture. We derive the classical **Rayleigh criterion** below. ### Step 1: Define the Imaging Condition When light of wavelength $\lambda$ passes through a circular lens aperture, the **Airy disk** formed at the image plane has a radius given by: $$ r_{\text{Airy}} = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda \cdot f}{D} $$ where $f$ is the focal length and $D$ is the lens diameter. ### Step 2: Introduce the Numerical Aperture The **numerical aperture (NA)** of a lens is defined as: $$ \text{NA} = n \cdot \sin(\theta) = n \cdot \frac{D/2}{f} $$ where $n$ is the refractive index of the medium between lens and wafer, and $\theta$ is the half-angle of the cone of light collected by the lens. ### Step 3: Express the Airy Disk in Terms of NA Substituting $f/D = 1/(2\,\text{NA}/n)$ into the Airy radius expression: $$ \begin{aligned} r_{\text{Airy}} &= 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2 \cdot \text{NA}/n} \\ &= 0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda \cdot n}{\text{NA}} \end{aligned} $$ In air ($n = 1$), this simplifies to: $$ r_{\text{Airy}} \approx 0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}} $$ ### Step 4: Apply the Rayleigh Two-Point Criterion According to the **Rayleigh criterion**, two point sources are *just resolved* when the center of one Airy disk coincides with the first minimum of the other. The minimum line/space pitch $W_{\text{min}}$ is therefore: $$ W_{\text{min}} = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}} $$ where $k_1$ is an empirically determined process factor that accounts for resist chemistry, illumination coherence, and image enhancement techniques (e.g., phase-shift masks, optical proximity correction). ### Step 5: Derive the Depth of Focus The **depth of focus (DOF)** is the axial range over which the image remains acceptably sharp. For a diffraction-limited system: $$ \text{DOF} = \pm \, k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** Higher NA improves resolution (smaller features) but **reduces DOF** quadratically. This is the central trade-off in lithography tool design. **Immersion lithography** (water between lens and wafer, $n = 1.44$) effectively increases NA without changing the air-gap geometry. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A MEMS foundry uses a 248 nm KrF excimer laser stepper with $\text{NA} = 0.65$ and $k_1 = 0.45$. Compute the minimum printable feature size and the depth of focus. Take $k_2 = 1.0$. ### Step 1: Substitute into the resolution equation $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{min}} &= k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}} \\ &= 0.45 \cdot \frac{248 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}}{0.65} \\ &= 0.45 \cdot 3.815 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \\ &= 1.717 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \\ &\approx 171.7 \, \text{nm} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute into the DOF equation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DOF} &= k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2} \\ &= 1.0 \cdot \frac{248 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}}{(0.65)^2} \\ &= \frac{248 \times 10^{-9}}{0.4225} \\ &= 5.870 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \\ &\approx 0.587 \, \mu\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Interpretation The system can print features down to ~172 nm, but the **depth of focus is only ~0.59 µm**. This means the wafer surface must be **planarized to within ~0.6 µm** across the entire field — a non-trivial requirement for MEMS substrates with topographical features. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Photoresist The decision matrix below helps select the appropriate photoresist for a given MEMS application. | **Application** | **Resist Type** | **Reason** | **Trade-off** | |---|---|---|---| | Sub-micron CMOS gate patterning | **Positive (DUV, 193 nm)** | High resolution, no swelling | Expensive, complex chemistry | | Thick SU-8 microchannels (>50 µm) | **Negative (SU-8)** | High aspect ratio, vertical sidewalls | Hard to strip after cure | | Quick academic lab demos | **Positive (S1813, g-line)** | Cheap, easy to process | ~1 µm resolution limit | | Metal lift-off stencil | **Positive, single-layer or image-reversal** | Undercut profile for clean lift-off | Requires precise dose control | | Dry-etch mask (DRIE, ICP) | **Negative (SU-8) or thick positive** | Excellent plasma resistance | Stripping is difficult | --- ## 3.4 Energy Coupling in Exposure The **exposure dose** $D$ delivered to the photoresist is the product of the **light intensity** $I$ at the wafer plane and the **exposure time** $t_{\text{exp}}$: $$ D = I \cdot t_{\text{exp}} \quad \left[ \text{mJ/cm}^2 \right] $$ For a positive resist, an **exposure threshold** $D_0$ exists below which the resist remains insoluble. The relationship between resist dissolution rate and dose follows the **Dill model**: $$ \frac{\partial M(x,t)}{\partial t} = -C \cdot I(x,t) \cdot M(x,t) $$ where $M(x,t)$ is the local concentration of the photoactive compound (PAC) at depth $x$ and time $t$, and $C$ is the **Dill parameter** (a material constant). > [!TIP] > For a fully coupled exposure–post-exposure-bake–development system, the **final pattern profile** can be predicted by combining the Dill exposure model with the **Mack resist development model**. This is a standard topic in advanced lithography courses. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Photolithography Process Flow The Mermaid diagram below maps the **full process pipeline** for a positive-resist photolithography sequence, including pre- and post-processing branches. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Start: Bare Wafer] --> B1[Step 1: Wafer Cleaning] B1 --> B2[Step 2: Dehydration Bake] B2 --> C1[Step 3: HMDS Priming] C1 --> D1[Step 4: Spin Coat Photoresist] D1 --> E1[Step 5: Soft Bake] E1 --> F1[Step 6: Mask Alignment] F1 --> G1[Step 7: UV Exposure] G1 --> H1[Step 8: Post Exposure Bake] H1 --> I1[Step 9: Development in TMAH] I1 --> J1[Step 10: Hard Bake] J1 --> K1[Patterned Resist Ready for Etch or Deposition] K1 --> L1{Process Loop?} L1 -- Yes --> B1 L1 -- No --> M1[Move to Next MEMS Module] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the flow:** Each rectangular block is a **process module**. The diamond `L1` is a **decision gate** asking whether the wafer needs to be re-patterned (e.g., for a multi-layer MEMS stack). If yes, the wafer returns to Step 1 of cleaning; if no, it exits to the next microfabrication step (etching, deposition, bonding, etc.). --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Projection Lithography Tool Architecture The diagram below illustrates the major subsystems of a modern step-and-scan projection lithography tool — the **industry standard** for sub-100 nm patterning. ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Illumination Source
Excimer Laser 193 nm] --> S2[Beam Shaping Optics
Collimator and Fly-Eye] S2 --> S3[Reticle Stage
Photomask Mounted] S3 --> S4[Projection Lens
High NA Objective] S4 --> S5[Wafer Stage
Precision XY Scanner] S5 --> S6[Alignment System
Laser Interferometer] S6 --> S7[Focus Control
Capacitive Sensors] S7 --> S8[Patterned Wafer Output] S6 -.feedback.-> S3 S7 -.feedback.-> S5 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dashed feedback lines** indicate **closed-loop control**. The alignment system continuously corrects the reticle position, and the focus system maintains the wafer within the depth of focus during scanning. This is why modern steppers achieve overlay accuracy of < 2 nm across a 300 mm wafer. --- ## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic: Positive vs. Negative Resist Patterning The following Mermaid flow depicts the **physical transformation** of the resist layer at each critical step, for both resist polarities. ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Substrate with Resist Film] --> P1[Apply Photomask] P1 --> P2[UV Exposure Through Mask] P2 --> P3[Chemical Change in Resist] P3 --> P4{Resist Polarity} P4 -- Positive --> P5[Exposed Regions Dissolve in Developer] P4 -- Negative --> P6[Unexposed Regions Dissolve in Developer] P5 --> P7[Final Pattern Matches Mask Openings] P6 --> P8[Final Pattern is Complement of Mask] ``` > [!TIP] > **Memory aid:** For **positive resist**, *"what you see (in the mask) is what you get."* For **negative resist**, the pattern is *inverted* relative to the mask openings. --- ## 4.4 Photolithography Subsystem Interaction Map A block-level architecture showing how the photolithography module interfaces with **adjacent MEMS fabrication steps**. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PreProcess["Pre-Lithography Modules"] M1[Oxidation or CVD Film Deposition] M2[Chemical Mechanical Polishing] end subgraph LithoCore["Photolithography Module"] L1[Coat] L2[Expose] L3[Develop] end subgraph PostProcess["Post-Lithography Modules"] N1[Wet or Dry Etching] N2[Physical Vapor Deposition] N3[Ion Implantation] N4[Wafer Bonding] end M1 --> L1 M2 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> N1 L3 --> N2 L3 --> N3 L3 --> N4 ``` > [!NOTE] > This block diagram emphasizes that **photolithography is a patterning service** consumed by other MEMS modules. The patterned resist dictates *where* the next process step modifies the substrate. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Photolithography. Differentiate between positive and negative photoresists. **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** > **Photolithography** is a microfabrication process in which a photosensitive polymer (photoresist) coated on a substrate is selectively exposed to ultraviolet light through a patterned mask, causing a chemical change that enables selective dissolution during development, thereby transferring a 2-D geometric pattern onto the substrate. > > | Aspect | Positive Photoresist | Negative Photoresist | > |---|---|---| > | Photochemistry | Chain scission | Cross-linking | > | Solubility after exposure | Exposed regions soluble | Exposed regions insoluble | > | Mask-pattern relation | Same as mask | Inverse of mask | > | Resolution | Higher (no swelling) | Lower (swelling) | > > **Example (1 mark):** AZ-5214E (positive); SU-8 (negative). ### Question 2: State the Rayleigh resolution criterion for photolithography and explain the role of numerical aperture (NA). **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** > The **Rayleigh criterion** defines the minimum printable half-pitch as: > > $$W_{\text{min}} = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}$$ > > where $\lambda$ is the exposure wavelength, $\text{NA} = n \sin\theta$ is the numerical aperture of the projection lens, and $k_1$ is a process factor. **Increasing NA sharpens the focused spot** (reduces Airy disk radius), thereby resolving finer features. The trade-off is a **quadratic reduction in depth of focus**, requiring tighter wafer flatness control. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Always state the **units** of every numerical answer. > - When asked to "explain" a step, **describe the chemistry or physics**, not just the name. > - On comparison questions, use a **table format** to avoid partial-credit ambiguity. > - On derivations, **do not skip intermediate steps**; partial credit depends on visible work. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **eight steps of the photolithography process**, including the purpose of each step, typical process parameters (temperature, time, chemicals), and the consequence of skipping or improperly executing each step. **(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a **schematic cross-section**, explain how a **positive photoresist** is patterned. Compare the resolution, etch resistance, and adhesion properties of positive vs. negative resists. #### Model Solution **Part (a) – Detailed Eight-Step Process** 1. **Wafer Cleaning (1 mark)** — Removes particulates, organics, and native oxide. Uses Piranha or RCA-1. **Consequence of skip:** pinholes, adhesion failure. 2. **Dehydration Bake (0.5 mark)** — 150 – 200 °C for 5 – 10 min to remove adsorbed water. **Consequence:** poor resist wetting. 3. **Priming with HMDS (1 mark)** — Vapour-phase deposition of HMDS forms hydrophobic Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃ layer. **Consequence:** resist delamination in developer. 4. **Photoresist Coating (1 mark)** — Spin-coat at 1,000 – 6,000 rpm; film thickness follows $t \propto 1/\omega^{\alpha}$. **Consequence of bad spin:** non-uniform thickness, edge beads. 5. **Soft Bake (0.5 mark)** — 90 – 120 °C, 60 s; removes solvent. **Consequence:** resist sticks to mask in contact mode; or under-exposes in projection. 6. **Mask Alignment & Exposure (1 mark)** — Align to alignment marks; expose at prescribed dose (mJ/cm²). **Consequence:** misalignment causes overlay error; under-exposure causes undeveloped regions. 7. **Post-Exposure Bake (1 mark)** — 100 – 130 °C, 60 s; reduces standing waves. **Consequence:** rough sidewalls, poor CD control. 8. **Development (1 mark)** — Immerse in TMAH (0.26 N) for 30 – 60 s; rinse in DI water. **Consequence:** over-development erodes features; under-development leaves resist residue. **Part (b) – Positive Resist Patterning & Comparison** **Cross-section description (3 marks):** - Start with a bare wafer (e.g., Si). - Coat with positive photoresist (e.g., S1813) to ~1 µm thickness. - Soft-bake, then expose to UV through a chrome-on-quartz mask. - Exposed regions undergo chain scission — the polymer (e.g., Novolac + DNQ PAC) becomes soluble in the basic developer (TMAH). - After development, only the unexposed regions remain, forming a 1:1 replica of the dark mask regions. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Property | Positive | Negative | |---|---|---| | **Resolution** | High (no swelling) | Lower (swelling in developer) | | **Etch resistance** | Moderate | Excellent (SU-8 is benchmark) | | **Adhesion to substrate** | Good (with HMDS) | Excellent (cross-linked) | | **Aspect ratio achievable** | Moderate (≤ 5:1 typical) | Very high (SU-8 > 20:1) | | **Stripping** | Easy (acetone) | Difficult (requires plasma or NMP) | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Rayleigh resolution limit** for a diffraction-limited projection lithography system, starting from the **Airy disk** formula. Define all variables and explain the physical significance of the numerical aperture and the process factor $k_1$. **(b) [7 Marks]** A MEMS foundry uses a stepper with $\lambda = 193 \, \text{nm}$ (ArF immersion), $n = 1.44$, $\text{NA} = 1.35$, and $k_1 = 0.35$. Compute (i) the **minimum printable feature size**, (ii) the **depth of focus** (with $k_2 = 1.0$), and (iii) the **minimum wafer flatness tolerance**. Comment on the engineering implications. #### Model Solution **Part (a) – Derivation (7 marks)** Starting from the Airy disk radius: $$ r_{\text{Airy}} = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda f}{D} $$ The numerical aperture is: $$ \text{NA} = n \cdot \sin\theta \approx n \cdot \frac{D/2}{f} $$ Solving for $f/D$: $$ \frac{f}{D} = \frac{n}{2 \, \text{NA}} $$ Substituting back: $$ \begin{aligned} r_{\text{Airy}} &= 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda \cdot n}{2 \, \text{NA}} \\ &= 0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda n}{\text{NA}} \end{aligned} $$ By Rayleigh's two-point criterion, the minimum resolvable half-pitch is proportional to $r_{\text{Airy}}$. Introducing the empirical process factor $k_1$: $$ W_{\text{min}} = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}} $$ **Physical significance (1 mark each):** - $\lambda$ — Shorter wavelength improves resolution (drives the industry to EUV). - NA — Larger collection angle sharpens the focal spot, improving resolution. - $k_1$ — Encapsulates resist chemistry, mask engineering, and resolution enhancement techniques (RETs) such as phase-shift masks, off-axis illumination, and optical proximity correction (OPC). Modern immersion lithography achieves $k_1 \approx 0.25$ — well below the classical 0.61 limit. **Part (b) – Numerical Computation (7 marks)** **(i) Minimum feature size:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{min}} &= k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}} \\ &= 0.35 \cdot \frac{193 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}}{1.35} \\ &= 0.35 \cdot 1.430 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \\ &= 5.00 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m} \\ &\approx 50 \, \text{nm} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Depth of focus:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DOF} &= k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2} \\ &= 1.0 \cdot \frac{193 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}}{(1.35)^2} \\ &= \frac{193 \times 10^{-9}}{1.8225} \\ &= 1.059 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \\ &\approx 106 \, \text{nm} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Minimum wafer flatness tolerance:** must be ≤ ± DOF/2, i.e., **± 53 nm** across the field. **Engineering implications (2 marks):** - The DOF is extraordinarily tight (~106 nm), demanding **atomic-scale wafer flatness** and active **focus control** during exposure. - The foundry must use **chemical-mechanical polishing (CMP)** to planarize the substrate prior to lithography. - Without resolution enhancement (low $k_1$), the system could not reach 50 nm features, illustrating the necessity of phase-shift masks and immersion optics. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Photolithography — Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Definition:** Light-driven transfer of a 2-D pattern from a photomask onto a photoresist-coated substrate. - **Eight steps:** Cleaning → Dehydration Bake → HMDS Priming → Spin Coating → Soft Bake → Alignment & Exposure → PEB → Development → (optional Hard Bake). - **Positive resist:** Exposed regions dissolve (e.g., AZ-5214E, S1813, PMMA). - **Negative resist:** Unexposed regions dissolve (e.g., SU-8, NR7-1500P). - **Exposure modes:** Contact, Proximity, Projection. Modern fabs use **projection step-and-scan** exclusively. - **Rayleigh equation:** $W_{\text{min}} = k_1 \cdot \lambda / \text{NA}$. - **Depth of focus:** $\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \lambda / \text{NA}^2$ — shrinks quadratically with NA. - **NA definition:** $\text{NA} = n \sin\theta$. Immersion lithography boosts NA by using $n > 1$ (e.g., water, $n = 1.44$). - **Key lithography wavelengths:** g-line (436 nm), h-line (405 nm), i-line (365 nm), KrF (248 nm), ArF (193 nm), EUV (13.5 nm). - **SU-8 is the MEMS workhorse** for thick, high-aspect-ratio structures (>100 µm). - **HMDS is essential** for resist adhesion — without it, developer undercuts the pattern. - **Trade-offs:** Higher NA → smaller features but tighter focus tolerance. - **Dill model + Mack model** quantitatively describe exposure and development kinetics. - **Photoresist thickness** follows a power-law dependence on spin speed: $t \propto 1/\omega^{\alpha}$. - **Soft bake, PEB, and hard bake** temperatures must be tightly controlled — over-bake causes resist flow; under-bake causes solvent-induced defects. - **Alignment accuracy** determines **overlay error**, the dominant metric for multi-layer MEMS. > **End of Photolithography Notes — Module 4, PEERT632, KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Ion Implantation, Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT632 - MICRO-ELECTRO-MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ion Implantation, Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ion Implantation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ion Implantation** is a low-temperature, vacuum-based, physical process of introducing energetic, charged dopant atoms (ions) of a desired species into a solid substrate (typically a silicon wafer) to modify its electrical, mechanical, or chemical properties.

In semiconductor and MEMS fabrication, ion implantation is the **dominant modern doping technique** because it offers precise control over both the **dose** (number of ions per unit area) and the **depth profile** of the implanted species — capabilities that classical diffusion cannot match.

**Conceptual Analogy — Intuition:**
Imagine you are standing 50 meters away from a large, thick wall of soft clay, holding thousands of small ball bearings. A slingshot fires the balls one by one into the wall.
- The *speed* at which you shoot each ball controls **how deep** it penetrates.
- The *total number* of balls you shoot controls **how many** end up inside.
